proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic and Functional Profiling Reveals Discrete, Tissue-Resident Subsets of Murine Natural Killer Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, NK cells are defined by a limited set of surface markers (e.g., NK1.1, Ly49) and considered a relatively homogeneous lineage of innate lymphocytes. However, emerging single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) technologies from 2014-2020 reveal profound heterogeneity in other immune cells. This proposal challenges the monolithic view of NK cells, hypothesizing they comprise distinct, stable subsets with unique developmental origins and functions, akin to T cell subsets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that murine NK cells are not a single cell type but consist of multiple, transcriptionally and functionally distinct subsets that arise from discrete developmental pathways within the bone marrow and are maintained in peripheral tissues.
proposals.method: We will use C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice. Bone marrow and splenic NK cells (NK1.1+CD3-) will be sorted and profiled via droplet-based scRNA-seq (10x Genomics). Data will be analyzed using Seurat (v4) for clustering, trajectory inference (Monocle3), and identification of differentially expressed genes. Candidate subset-defining genes (e.g., novel receptors, transcription factors) identified computationally will be validated using flow cytometry. The developmental requirement of Flt3 ligand for identified subsets will be tested using Flt3L-deficient mice and in vitro cultures of Flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive single-cell atlas of murine NK cell heterogeneity, defining novel subsets with unique transcriptional programs. It will link specific subsets to distinct developmental pathways from Flt3+ progenitors and propose a new framework for classifying NK cells based on core molecular signatures rather than a few activating/inhibitory receptors like 2B4.
proposals.novelty: While NK receptor diversity (e.g., 2B4 isoforms) is known, the idea that NK cells comprise fundamentally different cell types with separate developmental trajectories, rather than a continuum of states, is novel and challenges the foundational classification of the lineage.

proposals.title: Functional Non-MHC Restricted Cytotoxicity is the Defining Lineage Feature of Mature Natural Killer Cells
proposals.motivation: The definition of natural killer (NK) cells has historically relied on surface markers (e.g., NK1.1, Ly49 in mice) and developmental origin from bone marrow progenitors. However, pre-2026 work shows that markers like 2B4 have dual inhibitory/stimulatory roles, and flt3+ progenitors can give rise to diverse lineages. This creates ambiguity: are we defining a lineage by its ontogeny or its core effector function? Resolving this is critical for understanding NK cell identity in health, after bone marrow transplantation, and in novel cellular grafts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acquisition of stable, non-MHC restricted cytotoxic function, not progenitor origin or a specific receptor expression profile, is the essential and defining feature of the mature NK cell lineage.
proposals.method: We will use an in vivo functional selection assay in BALB/c mouse hosts. Flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells will be transduced with a lentiviral barcode library and transplanted into irradiated hosts. After full reconstitution, hosts will be challenged with MHC Class I-deficient RMA-S tumor cells. NK cells mediating tumor clearance will be isolated via FACS, and their progenitor barcodes will be deeply sequenced and compared to the barcodes of phenotypically defined (NK1.1+, Ly49+) but unchallenged NK cells and other lineages from the same hosts. This will test if functional NK cells clonally derive from a restricted progenitor pool or from diverse progenitors that converged on the cytotoxic function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional framework for NK cell lineage definition, separating core identity from variable receptor expression. It will clarify the developmental plasticity of flt3+ progenitors and establish whether 'NK cell' is a functional state achievable by multiple developmental paths, which has implications for interpreting host reconstitution data and designing NK cell-based grafts.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies characterize receptors or progenitor potential, this proposal directly tests if function dictates lineage identity in vivo by coupling a clonal tracking method with a functional selection pressure, moving beyond correlative phenotypic definitions.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of a Unified Nomenclature for Murine NK Cells and Their Progenitors Using In Vivo and In Vitro Models
proposals.motivation: The field of natural killer (NK) cell biology lacks a standardized, functionally relevant nomenclature for defining NK cell subsets and their developmental intermediates, particularly those derived from flt3+ multipotent progenitors. This hinders clear communication and comparison across studies. A proposed nomenclature based on surface receptor expression (e.g., Ly49, NK1.1, 2B4) and functional potential needs rigorous experimental validation to be widely adopted.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a proposed receptor-based nomenclature (integrating markers like NK1.1, Ly49, CD11b, and 2B4) to diverse NK cell populations from different anatomical sites and developmental stages will reveal consistent, functionally distinct subsets that correlate with cytotoxic potential and cytokine production.
proposals.method: We will use BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice to harvest NK cells from bone marrow, spleen, and liver. Cells will be analyzed by high-parameter flow cytometry for the proposed nomenclature markers (NK1.1, Ly49 isoforms, 2B4, CD11b, CD27). Sorted subsets, defined by the nomenclature, will be functionally tested in vitro for cytotoxicity against YAC-1 targets and IFN-γ production. Furthermore, we will test the nomenclature's applicability to developmental intermediates by analyzing and sorting flt3+ marrow progenitor cells cultured in NK-differentiating conditions, tracking the acquisition of the proposed subset signatures over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive experimental dataset testing a unified NK cell nomenclature in mice, linking surface phenotype directly to function across tissues and development. It will establish a benchmark for future studies on NK cell diversity and generate a validated gating strategy for the community.
proposals.novelty: While markers like NK1.1 and Ly49 are well-studied individually, systematically testing a combined, hierarchical nomenclature across development and function is novel. This approach directly builds on Kumar's expertise in NK receptors (2B4) and marrow-derived progenitors to solve a persistent classification problem.

proposals.title: Multi-Parameter Flow Cytometric Phenotyping of NK Cell Subsets in Murine Bone Marrow Grafts and Their Development in BALB/c Hosts
proposals.motivation: The developmental pathway from Flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitors to mature, receptor-expressing NK cells remains incompletely mapped, particularly in the context of bone marrow transplantation. Pre-2026, emerging high-parameter flow cytometry allows for the simultaneous detection of numerous surface markers, enabling a more precise dissection of NK cell subsets and their functional receptor profiles (e.g., 2B4, Ly49) during engraftment and differentiation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, phenotypically defined NK cell progenitor and mature subsets within bone marrow grafts can be identified by multi-parameter flow cytometry and that their representation and receptor expression profile (inhibitory vs. stimulatory 2B4) will be altered during engraftment and differentiation in BALB/c hosts.
proposals.method: We will use a 12-15 color flow cytometry panel on a modern cytometer (e.g., BD FACSymphony) to analyze and sort cells from murine bone marrow grafts and recipient spleens/bone marrow. The panel will include markers for lineage exclusion (CD3, CD19), progenitor status (Flt3, c-Kit, IL-7Rα), NK cell commitment (NK1.1, CD122), maturation (CD11b, CD27), and key receptors (Ly49 family isoforms, 2B4). Gated populations will be sorted for functional assays and transcriptional analysis. Data will be analyzed using FlowJo software with t-SNE or PCA for high-dimensional visualization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-resolution phenotypic map of NK cell development from graft-derived progenitors in vivo. It will directly link the expression of specific receptor forms, like 2B4, to defined developmental stages, offering mechanistic insight into how the graft environment shapes the emerging NK cell repertoire. This will advance our understanding of immune reconstitution post-transplantation.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kumar's prior work characterized 2B4 and Ly49 receptors and NK cell differentiation in isolation, this proposal integrates these themes by applying emerging high-parameter flow cytometry to track these molecules simultaneously on developing NK cell subsets within the specific physiological context of bone marrow grafts and hosts.

proposals.title: Genetic Fate-Mapping of flt3+ Marrow Progenitors Reveals a Direct Lineage Commitment Pathway to Mature NK1.1+Ly49+ Natural Killer Cells
proposals.motivation: Despite the identification of flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitors as a source of NK cells, the precise lineage trajectory and commitment point from this progenitor pool remain unclear. Resolving this ontogeny is critical for understanding NK cell development in health and for optimizing NK cell-based immunotherapies derived from hematopoietic grafts. Current models, based on transplantation and in vitro assays, lack the resolution to track clonal fate in an unperturbed physiological setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of bone marrow flt3+ progenitors undergoes direct, irreversible commitment to the NK cell lineage, giving rise to mature NK1.1+Ly49+ cells without passing through a T/NK bipotent stage in vivo.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-recombinase fate-mapping system using Flt3-Cre and Rosa26-lox-stop-lox-tdTomato mice crossed with a novel NK lineage reporter strain (e.g., Ncr1-iCre; Rosa26-lox-stop-lox-EGFP). This allows permanent, sequential labeling of flt3+ progenitors and their NK-committed progeny. Single-cell transcriptomics (10x Genomics) and flow cytometric analysis of bone marrow, blood, and spleen from these mice will delineate the developmental intermediates. Clonal relationships will be inferred using computational lineage tracing algorithms, such as URD or Monocle 3, applied to the single-cell RNA-seq data to reconstruct the differentiation trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct in vivo genetic evidence for the lineal pathway from flt3+ progenitors to mature NK cells, defining key transcriptional checkpoints. It will clarify whether NK cell development is a direct, linear process or involves divergent branches from a common lymphoid progenitor, fundamentally refining the hematopoietic tree.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Kumar et al. used in vitro differentiation and transplantation, this proposal utilizes an in vivo dual-recombinase genetic fate-mapping strategy combined with single-cell genomics, a non-transplant approach that preserves the native bone marrow niche for unambiguous lineage tracing.

proposals.title: Negative Definition of Natural Killer Cells: A Systematic Exclusion of T-Lineage Markers Reveals a Novel NK Progenitor and Functional Subset
proposals.motivation: Natural Killer (NK) cells are classically defined by a positive set of markers (e.g., NK1.1, CD49b, absence of CD3). However, this definition is confounded by NK-like T cells and innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) that share overlapping phenotypes. A rigorous, negative definition based on the systematic exclusion of T-cell lineage commitment markers is needed to isolate a pure, developmentally distinct NK cell population and resolve its ontogeny from flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hematopoietic progenitor population and its mature NK cell progeny can be uniquely identified and functionally characterized by the sequential and comprehensive exclusion of markers associated with T-cell receptor rearrangement and T-lineage commitment.
proposals.method: We will use BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice. Bone marrow progenitor cells (flt3+, Lin-) will be sorted using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) through a sequential gating strategy that negatively excludes all known T-lineage markers (CD3ε, TCRβ, TCRγδ, CD5, CD4, CD8α, CD8β, CD25 during activation). The resulting 'T-not' NK progenitor and mature NK populations will be assessed for NK receptor expression (Ly49, NKR-P1, 2B4), cytotoxicity against YAC-1 targets, and cytokine production. Their developmental potential will be tested in vivo using adoptive transfer into irradiated syngeneic hosts and in vitro using OP9-DL1 co-cultures to challenge T-lineage exclusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, stringent immunophenotypic definition for NK cells, clarifying their developmental pathway independent of the T lineage. It will identify a putative 'T-not' NK progenitor and may reveal a functionally distinct NK subset, providing a clearer framework for studying NK cell biology, receptor function (e.g., 2B4), and the therapeutic potential of NK cell grafts.
proposals.novelty: While NK cells are defined by the absence of CD3, a comprehensive, systematic negative selection against the full suite of T-lineage markers has not been applied to their ontogeny. This inverse approach is distinct from positive selection strategies and directly tests the developmental independence of the NK lineage.

proposals.title: Defining the Sufficiency of IL-15 for the Terminal Maturation of Natural Killer Cells from Committed Progenitors
proposals.motivation: IL-15 is a critical cytokine for NK cell development and survival, but its precise role in the final maturation stages remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 studies suggest other cytokines like IL-2, IL-12, or IL-18 may be required for full functional maturation. Clarifying if IL-15 is the sole extrinsic factor needed after the NK cell precursor (NKP) stage is essential for understanding developmental checkpoints and for optimizing ex vivo NK cell generation for immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IL-15 signaling is necessary and sufficient to drive the complete terminal maturation of committed NK cell progenitors into functionally competent, mature NK cells expressing a full repertoire of activating and inhibitory receptors.
proposals.method: We will isolate lineage-negative, Sca-1+, c-Kit+, CD122+, CD27+ NK cell progenitors from the bone marrow of C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice. These cells will be cultured on OP9 stromal cells expressing the Notch ligand Delta-like 1 (OP9-DL1) to support early commitment, followed by transfer to a stromal-free system. The final maturation phase will be tested in media containing only IL-15. Maturation will be assessed by weekly flow cytometry for NK1.1, CD11b, CD27, CD43, KLRG1, and a panel of Ly49 receptors. Functional competence will be tested via cytotoxicity against YAC-1 targets and cytokine production after stimulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the cytokine requirements for the final stage of NK cell maturation, resolving a key uncertainty in the developmental pathway. It will provide a simplified, defined protocol for generating mature NK cells from precursors, which is directly applicable to cell therapy manufacturing. The findings will also inform models of how cytokine signals integrate with receptor acquisition during terminal differentiation.
proposals.novelty: While IL-15's necessity is known, its sufficiency for complete terminal maturation—including the acquisition of a diverse Ly49 receptor repertoire and full cytolytic function—has not been rigorously tested in a defined, stromal-free system starting from highly purified committed progenitors.

proposals.title: Functional Profiling of In Vitro-Derived NK1.1+ Ly49- Cells Reveals a Distinct Effector Phenotype Independent of Classic NK Cell Maturation
proposals.motivation: In murine natural killer (NK) cell development, the acquisition of Ly49 receptors is considered a hallmark of functional maturity. However, the functional capacity of the NK1.1+ Ly49- population, particularly those generated from in vitro culture systems like flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells, remains poorly defined. Determining if these cells possess unique or alternative effector functions is critical for understanding NK cell developmental pathways and the functional heterogeneity of the innate immune compartment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vitro-derived NK1.1+ Ly49- cells are not simply immature precursors but possess a distinct, alternative functional program, potentially including cytokine production and non-cytolytic activities, independent of classic Ly49-mediated licensing.
proposals.method: We will generate NK1.1+ Ly49- cells by culturing flt3+ bone marrow progenitor cells from BALB/c mice in established cytokine cocktails (IL-15, SCF, Flt3L). These cells will be FACS-sorted and functionally compared to mature splenic NK1.1+ Ly49+ NK cells. Assays will include standard 4-hour chromium-51 release against YAC-1 and RMA-S targets, intracellular cytokine staining for IFN-γ and TNF-α after PMA/ionomycin or IL-12/IL-18 stimulation, and degranulation (CD107a) assays. Receptor expression (e.g., 2B4, NKG2D, DNAM-1) will be characterized by flow cytometry to correlate with function.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the functional potential of a major in vitro-derived NK cell subset, challenging the binary view of Ly49- cells as purely immature. It will establish whether the in vitro differentiation system produces cells with a unique functional identity, informing the use of such cells for immunotherapeutic grafts. The data will also refine models of NK cell development by dissociating phenotypic markers from functional competence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from this lab characterized inhibitory/stimulatory receptors like 2B4 and defined progenitor potential, no study has systematically tested the functional capacity of in vitro-generated Ly49- NK cells beyond cytotoxicity, nor questioned if they represent an alternative lineage rather than an arrested developmental stage.

proposals.title: Stromal Regulation of NK Cell Maturation: Defining the Role of the OP9 Cell Line in the Development of Ly49+ Natural Killer Cells from Murine Bone Marrow Progenitors
proposals.motivation: Understanding the stromal signals that drive natural killer (NK) cell development, particularly the acquisition of functionally critical Ly49 receptors, remains a key gap in immunology. Prior work from this lab and others has defined progenitor populations and receptor biology, but robust, reproducible in vitro systems to study the later stages of NK cell maturation, especially in mice, are limited. The OP9 stromal cell line, widely used for T and B cell differentiation, offers a promising but underexplored platform for NK cell development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the OP9 stromal cell line provides a supportive microenvironment sufficient to drive the complete in vitro maturation of flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells into functional, Ly49-expressing NK cells. We further hypothesize that OP9-derived signals will regulate the expression of key receptors like 2B4 during this process.
proposals.method: We will isolate lineage-negative, flt3+ hematopoietic progenitor cells from the bone marrow of C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice. These cells will be co-cultured on confluent layers of OP9 stromal cells in the presence of cytokines (IL-15, SCF, Flt3L). Cultures will be analyzed weekly by flow cytometry for the sequential acquisition of NK1.1, CD122, 2B4, and the Ly49 family of receptors. Functional maturation will be assessed via cytotoxicity assays against YAC-1 targets and intracellular IFN-γ production. Control cultures without OP9 stroma will be run in parallel.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, standardized in vitro system for studying murine NK cell development, enabling precise dissection of stromal-derived signals. It will directly link the Kumar lab's expertise in NK progenitor biology and receptor characterization to a powerful differentiation model. The findings will provide foundational knowledge for subsequent studies on stromal defects in immunodeficiency or for generating NK cells for adoptive immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While OP9 cells are established for lymphoid lineage differentiation, their specific utility for supporting the full maturation program of NK cells, including the stochastic and education-dependent expression of Ly49 receptors, has not been systematically demonstrated. This approach integrates a classic stromal tool with contemporary understanding of NK developmental checkpoints.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic and Proteomic Profiling Reveals a Continuum of Maturation Marker Expression in Murine Natural Killer Cell Precursors
proposals.motivation: The developmental pathway from hematopoietic stem cells to mature natural killer (NK) cells is defined by the sequential acquisition of surface receptors like NK1.1 and Ly49. Current models, based on flow cytometry, depict discrete stages where precursors completely lack these markers. This binary view may oversimplify the biology and obscure a more continuous differentiation process, potentially missing key transitional states critical for understanding NK cell development in health and after bone marrow transplantation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that murine NK cell precursors, including flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells, express low but biologically significant levels of mature NK cell markers (e.g., NK1.1, Ly49, 2B4) prior to their conventional identification as mature cells, and that this low-level expression defines a continuum of maturation.
proposals.method: We will use C57BL/6 and congenic BALB/c mice as hosts and donors for bone marrow chimeras to study development in vivo. Highly enriched populations of lineage-negative, flt3+ bone marrow progenitor cells and downstream NK precursors (e.g., pre-proNK, rNKP) will be isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS). We will employ high-parameter (28+ color) spectral flow cytometry with optimized staining protocols to detect low-abundance antigens. This will be integrated with single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics platform) on sorted populations to correlate transcript levels of genes like *Klrb1c* (NK1.1) and *Klra* family members (Ly49) with surface protein expression, identifying transitional states.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the stage-based paradigm of NK cell development by providing evidence for a continuum model. It will establish more sensitive methods for detecting early NK lineage commitment and define novel precursor populations based on low-level marker expression. This refined map will inform studies on NK cell deficiencies and the reconstitution of functional NK cells from grafts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kumar and others characterized the function of mature receptors like 2B4, the explicit search for their low-level expression on defined multipotent progenitors (flt3+) using integrated scRNA-seq and high-sensitivity flow cytometry is novel. It directly tests a fundamental assumption in the field's staging schemes.

proposals.title: A Systematic Comparison of In Vivo Lung Clearance and In Vitro Cytotoxicity Assays Reveals Divergent Sensitivity to NK Cell Receptor Signaling Pathways
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell function is assessed using both in vitro (e.g., 51Cr-release) and in vivo (e.g., lung clearance of MHC-I-deficient target cells) assays, but their correlation and mechanistic underpinnings are poorly defined. This gap is critical as pre-2026 studies, including Kumar's work on receptors like 2B4, show these pathways can differentially affect cytotoxicity and cytokine production, potentially leading to assay-dependent conclusions about NK cell efficacy in grafts and tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in vivo lung clearance assay is more sensitive to deficiencies in specific activating receptor pathways (e.g., Ly49H, NKG2D) and adhesion molecules required for extravasation and target engagement, whereas the in vitro lysis assay primarily reflects the final cytolytic machinery and is more sensitive to direct inhibitory receptor signaling.
proposals.method: We will use BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice as hosts and marrow donor backgrounds. NK cells from wild-type and genetically deficient mice (e.g., 2B4-/-, DAP10-/-, β2-integrin-/-) will be tested in parallel. In vitro lysis will be measured against YAC-1 and RMA-S targets in a standard 4-hour 51Cr-release assay. In vivo clearance will be quantified by intravenous injection of CFSE-labeled, MHC-I-deficient target cells and flow cytometric analysis of lung homogenates 4 hours later. Data will be analyzed using non-linear regression models to compare dose-response curves and EC50 values for NK cell numbers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, quantitative framework for interpreting NK cell functional data across assay platforms, clarifying discrepancies in the literature. It will identify which receptor pathways are most critical for in vivo effector function, directly informing the design of NK cell-based therapies and the evaluation of marrow-derived NK cell grafts.
proposals.novelty: While both assays are commonly used, a head-to-head mechanistic dissection linking specific receptor deficiencies (like 2B4) to differential assay outcomes has not been systematically performed, moving beyond simple correlation to establish causal, pathway-specific assay sensitivity.

proposals.title: Quantitative Limiting Dilution Analysis of Natural Killer Cell Precursors in Murine Bone Marrow Grafts
proposals.motivation: The precise frequency and developmental potential of natural killer (NK) cell precursors within hematopoietic grafts is poorly quantified, limiting our understanding of NK cell reconstitution post-transplantation. Pre-2026 work, including our own on NK cell differentiation from flt3+ progenitors and the characterization of receptors like 2B4, has defined key cellular and molecular players but lacks rigorous quantification of precursor frequency. A precise measurement is critical for optimizing NK cell-based immunotherapies and understanding developmental bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the frequency of functional NK cell precursors within BALB/c mouse bone marrow can be accurately determined using limiting dilution analysis (LDA) in vitro, and that this frequency is modulated by the expression of key receptors such as 2B4 on progenitor cells.
proposals.method: We will perform LDA by plating serial dilutions of lineage-depleted (e.g., Lin-) bone marrow cells from BALB/c mice into 96-well plates containing irradiated OP9 stromal cells and a cytokine cocktail (IL-15, Flt3L, SCF). After 21 days, wells will be scored for NK cell outgrowth (NK1.1+, CD3-) by flow cytometry. The precursor frequency will be calculated using the Poisson distribution and the minimum chi-square method. We will further fractionate progenitors based on 2B4 expression prior to LDA to assess its impact on precursor frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first precise, quantitative measure of functional NK cell precursor frequency in a standard murine model, establishing a benchmark for future studies. It will directly link our molecular studies on receptors like 2B4 to a quantifiable developmental output, offering a new functional assay to evaluate how genetic or therapeutic manipulations affect the NK cell progenitor pool.
proposals.novelty: While LDA is a classic technique, its systematic application to quantify NK cell precursors from defined bone marrow grafts, integrated with pre-fractionation based on specific receptor expression (2B4), is novel. This moves beyond descriptive phenotyping to a rigorous functional quantification not commonly applied to NK cell development at this time.

proposals.title: Identification of Marrow Stromal-Derived Soluble Factors Regulating NK Cell Lineage Commitment Using a Blocking Monoclonal Antibody Approach
proposals.motivation: The molecular signals from the bone marrow stroma that drive the commitment of Flt3+ multipotent progenitors to the NK cell lineage remain poorly defined. Elucidating these signals is critical for understanding NK cell development and for improving NK cell-based immunotherapies. A targeted approach to block and identify these specific stromal factors is currently lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, soluble factors produced by primary murine bone marrow stromal cells are essential for the differentiation of Flt3+ progenitors into mature NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells, and that these factors can be identified by generating monoclonal antibodies that block this developmental process.
proposals.method: We will generate a panel of monoclonal antibodies against membrane and secreted proteins from the OP9 stromal cell line and primary BALB/c mouse marrow stroma. These antibodies will be screened in co-culture assays where Flt3+ marrow progenitor cells from C57BL/6 mice are differentiated on stromal layers. Antibodies that inhibit the generation of NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells, as measured by flow cytometry, will be used to immunoprecipitate their target antigens from stromal cell supernatants or lysates for identification by mass spectrometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel stromal-derived molecular cues critical for NK cell lineage specification. It will provide a set of validated blocking tools to dissect the temporal requirements of these signals during development. The findings will directly inform strategies to expand or manipulate NK cells ex vivo for adoptive cell therapy.
proposals.novelty: While stromal co-cultures are standard, this approach actively uses function-blocking antibodies as discovery tools to pinpoint specific soluble factors, moving beyond descriptive cytokine screens or genetic knockout models of stromal cells.

proposals.title: Induction of a Progenitor-like State in Mature Murine NK Cells via Defined Cytokine and Stromal Co-culture
proposals.motivation: While NK cell development from hematopoietic progenitors is well-studied, the plasticity and potential dedifferentiation of mature NK cells remain unexplored. Understanding if mature NK cells can revert to a precursor state could reveal new mechanisms of immune cell plasticity and inform strategies for generating NK cells for immunotherapy from alternative, more accessible sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mature NK cells, when exposed to specific cytokine combinations and stromal microenvironments, can downregulate terminal differentiation markers, re-express progenitor-associated genes, and regain multipotent differentiation potential.
proposals.method: We will isolate mature NK1.1+ Ly49+ NK cells from C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice. These cells will be co-cultured on OP9 or OP9-DL1 stromal layers with cytokine cocktails containing Flt3L, SCF, and IL-15/IL-7. Cell fate will be tracked using a Confetti multicolor reporter system. Phenotypic reversion will be assessed by flow cytometry for progenitor markers (e.g., Flt3, c-Kit, CD27) and loss of Ly49. Functional potential will be tested by transferring cultured cells into sublethally irradiated syngeneic hosts or Rag2-/-γc-/- mice and analyzing donor-derived reconstitution of NK and other lymphoid lineages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental model for NK cell dedifferentiation, defining the necessary signals. It will challenge the paradigm of NK cell lineage commitment as strictly unidirectional and could identify a novel, tractable progenitor population for cell engineering.
proposals.novelty: This directly inverts the established differentiation pathway studied in Kumar's prior work. The concept of mature lymphocyte dedifferentiation is largely unexplored, especially for NK cells, making the investigation of its feasibility and mechanisms fundamentally novel.

proposals.title: Engineering a Humanized Murine Innate Immune System via Flt3+ Progenitor Grafts to Model NK Cell Receptor Function in Cancer Immunotherapy
proposals.motivation: Preclinical cancer models often prioritize accurate tumor histology but rely on immunodeficient mouse hosts, neglecting a functional, human-relevant immune system. This limits the study of critical immune-tumor interactions, particularly for Natural Killer (NK) cells, whose activity is governed by a balance of inhibitory and stimulatory receptors like 2B4. A model with a fully reconstituted, humanized innate immune compartment is needed to accurately test NK cell-based immunotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engrafting human Flt3+ multipotent progenitor cells into immunodeficient BALB/c hosts will generate a stable, functional human innate immune system, including NK cells expressing key receptors, thereby creating a superior model for evaluating NK cell-targeted cancer therapies.
proposals.method: We will isolate human CD34+ Flt3+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) from cord blood. These cells will be transplanted into sub-lethally irradiated adult NOD-scid IL2Rγnull (NSG) mice on a BALB/c background. Engraftment and differentiation will be tracked over 12-16 weeks via flow cytometry for human NK cell markers (NK1.1, Ly49 homologs, 2B4) and functional assays (cytotoxicity, cytokine production). The model will be validated using tumors expressing ligands for the inhibitory and stimulatory forms of 2B4, characterized in Kumar's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, tractable in vivo platform to study human NK cell development and receptor signaling in an intact system. It will directly enable the testing of therapies that modulate NK cell receptors (e.g., 2B4-blocking or -agonistic antibodies) against tumors, moving beyond simplistic in vitro co-culture systems.
proposals.novelty: While humanized mouse models exist, they typically focus on adaptive immunity. This proposal uniquely prioritizes the engineering of a complete human innate immune compartment from a defined progenitor source (Flt3+), specifically to model the nuanced receptor-ligand interactions critical for NK cell anti-tumor function.

proposals.title: Comparative analysis of natural killer cell precursors reveals a unique, tissue-restricted progenitor population in fetal liver distinct from adult bone marrow
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell development is classically described as occurring primarily in the bone marrow (BM), with BM containing all necessary precursors. However, pre-2026 studies indicate that NK cells can develop in extra-medullary sites like the thymus and liver, particularly during fetal life, suggesting potential heterogeneity in developmental pathways. Determining whether these sites harbor unique, lineage-restricted precursors not found in adult BM is crucial for understanding the ontogeny and functional diversity of the NK cell compartment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fetal liver contains a distinct, tissue-restricted NK cell precursor population that is not present in the adult bone marrow, representing an alternative developmental pathway for NK cell generation.
proposals.method: We will use fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) to isolate lineage-negative, c-Kit+, Sca-1+, Flt3+ (LSK Flt3+) multipotent progenitor cells and more committed NK precursors (e.g., NKP: Lin-, CD122+, CD49b-, NK1.1-) from adult BALB/c mouse bone marrow and embryonic day 14.5 (E14.5) fetal liver. Sorted populations will be adoptively transferred into congenic, sub-lethally irradiated BALB/c hosts or injected into fetal liver organ cultures. The developmental potential, kinetics, and receptor repertoire (including Ly49 and 2B4) of progeny NK cells will be analyzed by flow cytometry and functional cytotoxicity assays. Competitive reconstitution experiments will directly compare the precursor activity from each source.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the long-standing paradigm that adult BM contains all NK precursors. It will establish whether the fetal liver houses a unique developmental niche with distinct progenitors, potentially explaining differences in fetal versus adult NK cell function and receptor repertoire. The findings will refine models of hematopoietic development and inform strategies for generating NK cells for immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kumar's prior work characterized NK cell differentiation from BM Flt3+ progenitors and 2B4 receptor biology, a direct, functional comparison of the precursor potential between the definitive hematopoietic site (adult BM) and a potent fetal site has not been comprehensively performed to confirm or refute the exclusivity of BM.

proposals.title: Sequential Acquisition of Qa-5, Thy-1, AsGm-1, and NK-1.1 Defines a Linear Progression Model for Murine Natural Killer Cell Maturation from Flt3+ Bone Marrow Progenitors
proposals.motivation: While the developmental origin of NK cells from bone marrow precursors is established, the precise order and timing of key surface marker acquisition during in vitro maturation remain poorly defined. A detailed map of this progression is critical for isolating discrete developmental intermediates, understanding the functional maturation of receptors like 2B4, and providing a benchmark for evaluating the fidelity of NK cell differentiation from engineered grafts or expanded progenitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that murine NK cell maturation from Flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells follows a defined, linear sequence of surface marker acquisition, beginning with Qa-5, followed sequentially by Thy-1, AsGm-1, and culminating in NK-1.1 expression.
proposals.method: We will isolate Flt3+ lineage-negative (Lin-) bone marrow progenitor cells from BALB/c mice and culture them in vitro with IL-15 and stem cell factor to drive NK lineage commitment. Using multi-parameter flow cytometry, we will perform daily time-course analyses to track the co-expression patterns of Qa-5, Thy-1, AsGm-1, and NK-1.1. Data will be analyzed using conventional manual gating and SPICE (Simplified Presentation of Incredibly Complex Evaluations) software to establish probabilistic progression pathways and define statistically significant sequential stages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-resolution, temporal map of key surface marker acquisition during murine NK cell development in culture. It will provide a foundational framework for future studies on the regulation of maturation stages and allow for the functional interrogation of discrete NK precursor populations defined by specific marker combinations.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies, including Kumar's work on 2B4, often characterize mature NK cell receptors or progenitor potential, but do not systematically define the surface phenotype progression linking these states. This proposal directly addresses this gap by applying detailed temporal phenotyping to a well-defined progenitor culture system.

proposals.title: In vivo BrdU pulse-chase kinetics reveal the dynamics of NK cell production from flt3+ marrow progenitors in reconstituted BALB/c hosts
proposals.motivation: Understanding the genesis and turnover of natural killer (NK) cells from bone marrow progenitors is crucial for immunology and transplantation. While Kumar's prior work identified flt3+ multipotent progenitors as a source of NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells, the in vivo kinetics of this production—including the timing, magnitude, and lifespan of newly generated NK cells in a reconstituted host—remain poorly defined. Quantifying these parameters is essential for optimizing hematopoietic grafts and understanding NK cell homeostasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo BrdU labeling will quantitatively define a rapid, sustained wave of NK cell production from engrafted flt3+ marrow progenitors, and that the subsequent chase period will reveal distinct turnover rates for NK cell subsets based on Ly49 and 2B4 receptor expression.
proposals.method: We will adapt established in vivo BrdU pulse-chase protocols to the study of NK cell genesis. Lethally irradiated BALB/c hosts will be reconstituted with purified flt3+ bone marrow progenitor cells. At peak reconstitution (e.g., 4-5 weeks post-transplant), mice will receive a defined pulse of BrdU via drinking water. Cohorts will be sacrificed at serial time points post-pulse (days 1-14). Bone marrow, spleen, and blood will be harvested, and cells will be stained for surface markers (NK1.1, Ly49 isoforms, 2B4, CD122) and intracellular BrdU. Flow cytometric analysis will quantify the percentage and absolute number of BrdU+ NK cells over time, defining production and turnover kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative measurement of the in vivo production rate and lifespan of NK cells derived from a defined flt3+ progenitor pool in a reconstitution model. It will establish a foundational kinetic framework for NK cell development post-transplant. Furthermore, correlating turnover with inhibitory (Ly49) and co-stimulatory (2B4) receptor expression may link developmental history to functional subset stability.
proposals.novelty: While BrdU labeling is a classic cell kinetics tool, its systematic application to track the genesis of NK cells from a defined progenitor source in vivo is novel. This approach moves beyond static phenotypic characterization to dynamic, temporal analysis of NK cell production, a key gap in Kumar's prior work on progenitor differentiation and receptor characterization.

proposals.title: Stromal-Dependent Maturation of Natural Killer Cells in Long-Term Bone Marrow Culture: Defining the Role of 2B4 and Ly49 Receptor Acquisition
proposals.motivation: While NK cell development from bone marrow precursors is established, the precise temporal and stromal-dependent signals guiding the functional maturation of NK cells, including the sequential acquisition of key receptors like Ly49 and 2B4, remain poorly defined. Long-term bone marrow culture (LTBMC) systems, which recapitulate the hematopoietic niche, offer a powerful but underutilized tool to dissect this process over weeks, bridging the gap between in vitro progenitor assays and in vivo models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific stromal cell lines in LTBMC provide a permissive microenvironment that supports the sequential differentiation of flt3+ multipotent progenitors into mature, functional NK cells, and that the acquisition of the inhibitory receptor 2B4 is a late-stage event critical for functional maturation.
proposals.method: We will establish LTBMCs using the OP9 or MS-5 stromal cell lines co-cultured with lineage-depleted, flt3+ bone marrow progenitors from C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice. Cultures will be maintained for 4-6 weeks with weekly analysis by flow cytometry for NK1.1, CD122, CD94, Ly49 family members (e.g., Ly49C/I, Ly49G2), and 2B4. Functional maturation will be assessed via cytotoxicity against YAC-1 targets and cytokine production. Receptor blockade using anti-2B4 monoclonal antibodies (clone 2B4) will test its role in late-stage differentiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed kinetic map of NK cell receptor acquisition and functional maturation in a defined stromal microenvironment. It will establish LTBMC as a robust model for studying NK cell development and clarify the developmental stage at which 2B4, a receptor with dual signaling capacity, is expressed and becomes functionally relevant.
proposals.novelty: While LTBMC is classic for myelopoiesis, its systematic application to dissect the stepwise maturation of NK cells, particularly integrating the roles of recently cloned receptors like 2B4 and Ly49, represents a novel synthesis of established culture techniques with modern molecular immunology.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Divergence of NK Cell Effector Programs: A Comparative Analysis of Cytotoxicity Against YAC-1 Tumor Targets Versus MCMV-Infected Cells
proposals.motivation: The YAC-1 murine lymphoma line is a canonical target for assessing 'natural' NK cell cytotoxicity in vitro, but its relevance for evaluating anti-viral immunity is unclear. Given the distinct receptor-ligand interactions (e.g., NKG2D, DNAM-1 for tumors; Ly49H for MCMV) and signaling pathways involved, reliance on YAC-1 lysis may misrepresent the functional capacity of NK cells primed for viral defense, a critical gap in interpreting pre-clinical models of infection and immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the molecular pathways and functional receptor requirements (e.g., 2B4, Ly49H) for NK cell-mediated lysis of murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV)-infected primary cells are distinct and non-overlapping with those required for lysis of the YAC-1 tumor cell line.
proposals.method: We will use C57BL/6 and Balb/c mice as hosts, comparing NK cells from wild-type, 2B4-deficient, and Ly49H-deficient animals. Primary NK cells will be expanded from flt3+ marrow progenitor cultures or isolated from spleens. Cytotoxicity will be measured in parallel 4-hour 51Cr-release assays against YAC-1 targets and against syngeneic embryonic fibroblasts infected with MCMV. We will block specific pathways using monoclonal antibodies against NKG2D, DNAM-1, 2B4, and Ly49H to define receptor requirements. Intracellular staining for IFN-γ and CD107a will assess functional divergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the validity of a standard assay in the field, providing a mechanistic map of the divergent effector programs used by NK cells against tumor versus viral targets. It will clarify whether YAC-1 lysis is a reliable surrogate for anti-viral cytotoxicity, impacting the interpretation of countless studies on NK cell development, memory, and therapy.
proposals.novelty: While YAC-1 is ubiquitously used, a systematic, side-by-side dissection of its relevance for modeling anti-viral cytotoxicity, leveraging Dr. Kumar's expertise in NK cell receptors like 2B4 and marrow-derived progenitors, has not been performed. This directly challenges an entrenched methodological assumption.

proposals.title: In Vivo Tracking of Adoptively Transferred NK Cells to the Lung Using Radioactive Labeling in a Murine Model
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cells are critical for immune surveillance and anti-tumor responses, but their in vivo migratory patterns, particularly to peripheral sites like the lung, are poorly characterized. Understanding NK cell trafficking is essential for optimizing adoptive cell therapies for lung metastases and infections. Pre-2026 methods for tracking adoptively transferred cells in real-time, especially to solid organs, remain limited and often lack quantitative spatial resolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adoptively transferred, ex vivo-expanded NK cells derived from flt3+ marrow progenitors will preferentially migrate to the lung parenchyma within 24-48 hours post-transfer, and that this migration can be quantitatively tracked and validated using a dual-modal radioactive labeling approach.
proposals.method: NK cells will be expanded in vitro from BALB/c mouse flt3+ bone marrow progenitor cells. Cells will be dually labeled with the gamma-emitting radioisotope Indium-111-oxine for in vivo tracking via single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) and a fluorescent dye (e.g., CFSE) for ex vivo validation. Labeled NK cells will be adoptively transferred into syngeneic BALB/c hosts. Whole-body SPECT/CT imaging will be performed at serial time points (2, 24, 48, 72h) to quantify lung-specific radioactivity. Post-imaging, lungs will be harvested for flow cytometric analysis using NK1.1 and Ly49 markers to correlate imaging signals with actual NK cell presence.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, quantitative protocol for tracking NK cell migration in vivo, providing critical kinetic and biodistribution data for lung-targeting immunotherapies. It will directly link non-invasive imaging signals with cellular identity and location, offering a new tool to evaluate the efficacy of NK cell grafts and engineered receptors in pre-clinical models.
proposals.novelty: While radioactive cell tracking is established, its application to track the specific migration of in vitro-derived, progenitor-expanded NK cells to a solid organ like the lung is novel. Combining SPECT quantification with progenitor-derived NK cells bridges Kumar's expertise in NK cell biology and marrow grafts with advanced in vivo imaging.

proposals.title: Functional Consequences of SLAM Homoassociation in Macrophage Polarization and B Cell Activation
proposals.motivation: The SLAM family of receptors, including 2B4, are critical immunomodulatory molecules, but their signaling is best characterized in NK and T cells. Pre-2026 work shows SLAM receptors can self-associate (homoassociate) via their extracellular domains, a process implicated in receptor clustering and signal initiation. The functional impact of this specific homoassociation mechanism in other immune cells, such as macrophages and B cells, which also express various SLAM members, remains unexplored, creating a significant gap in understanding immune regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SLAM homoassociation, mediated by its N-terminal IgV domain, provides a cell-intrinsic signal that influences macrophage functional polarization and modulates B cell receptor signaling thresholds.
proposals.method: We will utilize primary cells from Balb/c mice and established SLAM-deficient or SLAM-transgenic models. For macrophages, bone marrow-derived macrophages (BMDMs) will be generated from flt3+ progenitor grafts in irradiated hosts. Homoassociation will be induced using recombinant SLAM-Fc proteins or cross-linking antibodies, and its impact on M1/M2 polarization will be assessed via cytokine profiling and surface marker analysis. For B cells, splenic B cells will be co-stimulated via BCR and SLAM homoassociation, measuring proliferation, calcium flux, and differentiation. Key readouts will include phosphorylation of SAP and ERK adaptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel regulatory function for SLAM homoassociation beyond NK/T cell biology, specifically in innate macrophage responses and adaptive B cell activation. It will establish whether this mechanism is a conserved signaling paradigm across the immune system, potentially identifying new targets for modulating inflammation or humoral immunity.
proposals.novelty: While SLAM signaling is studied in lymphocytes, the direct functional interrogation of its homoassociation property in myeloid cells and B cells is novel. This moves beyond correlative expression studies to mechanistically test a specific molecular interaction in these cell types.

proposals.title: In vivo function of NK cells derived from flt3+ progenitors in murine models of cytomegalovirus infection and graft-versus-host disease
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cells are critical for antiviral immunity and immune regulation. While in vitro studies, including Dr. Kumar's work on NK cell differentiation from flt3+ marrow progenitors and receptor characterization, have defined developmental pathways and signaling, the functional capacity of these in vitro-derived NK cells in complex disease settings remains untested. Validating their activity in vivo is essential to assess their therapeutic potential for cell-based therapies against infection or immune dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NK cells differentiated in vitro from flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells will exhibit robust antiviral and immunoregulatory function in vivo, controlling murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV) replication and mitigating graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) in BALB/c hosts through mechanisms involving the 2B4 receptor pathway.
proposals.method: We will differentiate NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells from C57BL/6 flt3+ marrow progenitors using established cytokine cocktails (flt3L, IL-15, IL-7). These cells will be adoptively transferred into two BALB/c host models: 1) MCMV infection to assess viral control via plaque assay and NK cell infiltration, and 2) a major MHC-mismatched bone marrow transplantation model to induce GVHD, monitoring survival and clinical scores. In both models, we will compare wild-type NK cells to those from 2B4-deficient donors or will administer blocking antibodies against 2B4 to dissect its role in vivo.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the functional efficacy of a defined progenitor-derived NK cell product in established disease models, providing critical preclinical data. It will elucidate the in vivo role of the 2B4 receptor pathway in NK cell-mediated protection during infection and immunopathology, bridging molecular characterization to physiological function.
proposals.novelty: While NK cell biology is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates a defined in vitro developmental system (flt3+ progenitor-derived NK cells) with parallel in vivo disease models to provide a comprehensive functional assessment, moving beyond descriptive receptor characterization to therapeutic validation.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Conjugate Formation Defect in Murine NK Cell Progenitors: A Critical Re-evaluation of the YAC-1 Cytotoxicity Assay
proposals.motivation: The YAC-1 lymphoma line is a standard target for assessing murine Natural Killer (NK) cell cytotoxicity. However, this assay conflates target binding and post-binding lytic events. For NK cell progenitors or functionally arrested cells identified in bone marrow grafts, a failure to lyse YAC-1 is typically interpreted as a lytic defect, potentially masking an earlier, critical impairment in adhesion or immunological synapse formation. Pre-2026 work on receptors like 2B4 highlights the complexity of NK cell activation, necessitating a granular dissection of these functional stages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of phenotypically defined NK-1.1+ cells, particularly those derived from flt3+ multipotent progenitors in Balb/c mice, possesses a genuine binding or conjugate formation defect with YAC-1 targets that is obscured by the standard chromium-release assay, which only measures the final lytic outcome.
proposals.method: We will use flow cytometry-based conjugate assays to directly quantify binding between sorted NK-1.1+ Ly49+ cells (from wild-type and marrow-progenitor-derived populations in Balb/c hosts) and CFSE-labeled YAC-1 targets. This will be paired with single-cell calcium flux imaging to assess post-binding activation. The functional data will be correlated with expression levels of adhesion molecules (e.g., LFA-1) and activating receptors (including 2B4 and Ly49 family members) via intracellular staining and surface profiling. Experiments will compare progenitor-derived NK cells to mature splenic NK cells as a control.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a more precise framework for evaluating early NK cell development and dysfunction by separating binding from killing. It will clarify whether defects in marrow-derived NK cell grafts are due to failed target recognition or failed execution of lysis. The findings will refine the interpretation of in vivo and in vitro NK cell functional data, particularly in transplantation and developmental studies.
proposals.novelty: While the YAC-1 assay is ubiquitous, systematically deconstructing its readout to isolate conjugate defects in defined progenitor populations is novel. This approach directly tests the assumed sequence of events in NK cell cytotoxicity within a developmental context informed by Kumar's prior work on NK receptors and marrow progenitors.

proposals.title: Cell Cycle Profiling of 2B4-Mediated NK Cell Arrest in Murine Bone Marrow Grafts
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell function is tightly regulated by a balance of activating and inhibitory receptors, such as 2B4. Pre-2026 work by Kumar et al. has characterized 2B4's dual signaling capacity but the precise cellular mechanism by which its inhibitory form induces functional arrest in NK-1.1+ cells remains unclear. Determining if this arrest corresponds to a specific cell cycle phase would provide a fundamental mechanistic insight into NK cell regulation, with implications for understanding graft outcomes and immune evasion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engagement of the inhibitory isoform of the 2B4 receptor on murine NK-1.1+ cells induces a state of functional quiescence that is associated with a specific arrest in the G0/G1 phase of the cell cycle.
proposals.method: We will employ flow cytometric cell cycle analysis techniques standard in oncology. NK-1.1+ cells from Balb/c mice, either ex vivo or derived from flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cultures, will be stimulated via plate-bound anti-2B4 monoclonal antibodies (clone 2B4, known to induce inhibition). Cells will be fixed, permeabilized, and stained with Propidium Iodide (PI) for DNA content quantification. Data will be acquired on a flow cytometer and cell cycle distribution (sub-G1, G0/G1, S, G2/M) will be analyzed using Dean-Jett-Fox or Watson pragmatic algorithms in FlowJo software, comparing stimulated cells to isotype controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly link a defined inhibitory receptor signal to a specific cell cycle checkpoint in primary NK cells, a connection rarely explored pre-2026. It will establish whether 2B4-mediated arrest is a reversible quiescence or a deeper proliferative block, informing models of NK cell tolerance in marrow microenvironments and potential manipulation for cell therapies.
proposals.novelty: While cell cycle analysis is routine in cancer biology, its application to dissect the mechanistic basis of receptor-mediated arrest in primary innate lymphoid cells is novel. This approach moves beyond functional assays (cytotoxicity, cytokine production) to define the cell-intrinsic state of 'arrested' NK cells.

proposals.title: Quantitative Adhesion Dynamics of NK-1.1+ Cells: Adapting Neutrophil Static and Flow Assays to Define 2B4 Receptor Function
proposals.motivation: Natural Killer (NK) cell adhesion to target cells is a critical, rate-limiting step in cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion, yet it remains poorly quantified. While the dual-function receptor 2B4 has been characterized in NK cell activation and inhibition, its precise role in mediating the physical adhesion step is unclear. A quantitative framework, akin to established assays for neutrophil-endothelial interactions, is needed to dissect the molecular mechanics of NK cell arrest and synapse formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the adhesion strength and kinetics of murine NK-1.1+ cells to target cells can be precisely measured using adapted neutrophil adhesion assays, and that these parameters are differentially modulated by the stimulatory versus inhibitory isoforms of the 2B4 receptor.
proposals.method: We will adapt two quantitative assays from neutrophil research. First, a static parallel-plate flow chamber assay will quantify the shear resistance of NK-1.1+ cells (isolated from C57BL/6 or BALB/c mice or derived from flt3+ marrow progenitors) adhering to 2B4 ligand (CD48)-expressing target cell monolayers (e.g., RMA-S). Second, a microfluidic-based live-cell imaging assay under controlled laminar flow will measure the rolling velocity, arrest time, and firm adhesion of NK cells. Experiments will be performed using NK cells from wild-type and 2B4-deficient hosts, with and without receptor blockade using monoclonal antibodies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, biophysical profile of NK cell adhesion, defining metrics like critical shear stress for detachment. It will directly link the molecular function of the 2B4 receptor to a physical adhesion outcome, clarifying its role in the cytolytic synapse. The adapted methodology will provide a new toolkit for the NK cell field to evaluate other adhesion receptors.
proposals.novelty: While adhesion is acknowledged as important, applying rigorous, quantitative biophysical assays standard in neutrophil research to NK cells is novel. Furthermore, directly testing how the signaling properties of 2B4 isoforms translate into quantifiable adhesion mechanics represents a new approach to understanding this receptor's function.

proposals.title: Epithelial Microenvironmental Cues Restrain Conventional T Cell Acquisition of Innate-like T-IEL Phenotype
proposals.motivation: Intraepithelial lymphocytes (IELs) in the gut contain a major population of innate-like T cells (e.g., TCRγδ+ and TCRαβ+ CD8αα+) with distinct effector-memory properties, while conventional T cells (Tconv) in the epithelium remain phenotypically distinct. Understanding the signals that prevent Tconv from adopting this resident IEL program is crucial for deciphering tissue-specific immune regulation and could inform therapies for barrier site pathologies like inflammatory bowel disease and celiac disease, where Tconv dysregulation is key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the gut epithelial microenvironment provides specific inhibitory signals, potentially via stromal or epithelial cell interactions and/or a defined cytokine milieu, that actively restrain conventional CD4+ and CD8αβ+ T cells from acquiring the innate-like, tissue-resident phenotype characteristic of canonical T-IELs.
proposals.method: We will use a reductionist in vivo model by adoptively transferring congenically marked, antigen-specific naive conventional T cells (OT-I, OT-II) into syngeneic BALB/c or C57BL/6 hosts. Recipients will be treated with FTY720 to prevent egress, enriching for epithelium-adapted cells. Transferred T cells isolated from the intestinal epithelium and lamina propria will be analyzed by high-parameter flow cytometry (for CD8αα, CD69, CD103, NK1.1, Ly49 receptors, and transcription factors like Hobit) and single-cell RNA sequencing. We will test the role of specific signals by using knockout hosts (e.g., for IL-15, TGF-β, or Notch ligands) or co-transfer experiments with bone marrow chimeras to dissect hematopoietic vs. stromal requirements.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the cellular and molecular checkpoints that maintain the phenotypic boundary between innate-like and conventional T cells within the same tissue niche. It will provide a functional map of the epithelial signals that constrain T cell adaptation, offering new targets for modulating tissue immunity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work defines signals promoting innate-like IEL development, this study directly tests the novel concept of active restraint on Tconv, leveraging Dr. Kumar's expertise in NK receptor biology (e.g., 2B4, Ly49) to investigate inhibitory pathways that may be shared across lymphoid lineages in epithelial niches.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Resolution of 2B4 (CD244) Expression Dynamics and Functional Consequences in Murine NK and T Cell Subsets
proposals.motivation: The 2B4 receptor is a critical regulator of NK and T cell function, but its expression is often described as constitutive. However, bulk analyses mask heterogeneity and dynamic regulation within complex immune cell populations. A precise, single-cell understanding of how 2B4 expression is modulated by activation state, cytokine milieu, and differentiation stage is lacking, as is a direct link between these expression dynamics and functional outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 2B4 expression is not constitutive but is dynamically regulated at the single-cell level in response to immune stimulation and polarization, and that these expression changes directly tune the signaling threshold and effector functions of NK and T cell subsets.
proposals.method: We will use BALB/c mice and in vitro differentiation from flt3+ marrow progenitors to generate NK cells. Cells will be subjected to ex vivo stimulation (e.g., anti-Ly49D, cytokines IL-12/IL-18) and polarization (IL-2, IL-15, TGF-β). High-parameter flow cytometry (20+ parameters) will quantify 2B4 protein density, co-receptors, and phospho-signaling proteins (e.g., p-ERK, p-SHP-1). Paired scRNA-seq (10x Genomics) will capture transcriptomic states and *Cd244* expression. Crucially, index-sorting will link these molecular profiles to functional assays (single-cell cytotoxicity via FACS-sorted effectors on YAC-1 targets, and multiplex cytokine secretion analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first integrated, multi-modal atlas of 2B4 regulation at single-cell resolution, defining its expression landscape across immune states. It will directly test the functional consequence of 2B4 expression variance, revealing whether it acts as a tunable rheostat for lymphocyte activation. This will clarify the receptor's role in immune modulation and potential therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, causal coupling of single-cell 2B4 expression dynamics—measured across protein, transcript, and surface density modalities—to quantifiable effector functions, moving beyond correlative bulk studies to define precise regulatory logic.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of the Cellular Ligand for the Murine 2B4 Receptor
proposals.motivation: The 2B4 receptor (CD244) is a critical regulator of natural killer (NK) cell function, capable of delivering both inhibitory and activating signals. However, its cellular ligand remains unidentified, creating a major gap in understanding the molecular basis of NK cell communication and target cell recognition. Identifying this ligand is essential for elucidating how 2B4 modulates NK cell responses in immunity, transplantation, and potentially cancer surveillance.
proposals.hypothesis: The ligand for the murine 2B4 receptor is a cell surface protein expressed on hematopoietic cells, and its interaction with 2B4 is responsible for modulating NK cell cytotoxicity and cytokine production.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach using established pre-2026 techniques. First, a soluble recombinant 2B4-Fc fusion protein will be used as a probe to screen a cDNA expression library from BALB/c splenocytes transfected into COS-7 cells, identifying binding partners. Second, we will use a cell-based binding assay with 2B4-transfected BW5147 cells to probe a panel of candidate hematopoietic cell lines (e.g., YAC-1, P815) and primary cells from C57BL/6 and BALB/c mice, including bone marrow grafts and activated T cells. Positive interactions will be confirmed by flow cytometry and cross-linking experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the cellular ligand for 2B4, resolving a long-standing question in NK cell biology. It will provide the foundation for understanding the physiological contexts of 2B4 signaling and enable future studies on manipulating this pathway in disease models, such as graft-versus-host disease and viral infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this lab and others has characterized the 2B4 receptor's signaling and expression, the identity of its natural ligand on opposing cells is unknown. This proposal directly targets this specific knowledge gap using classic, robust immunological methods rather than focusing solely on receptor biochemistry.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of the Human Homolog of the Murine NK Cell Receptor 2B4
proposals.motivation: The mouse 2B4 receptor is a critical CD2/SLAM family member that modulates NK cell cytotoxicity and cytokine production upon interaction with CD48. While a partial human sequence with homology to mouse 2B4 has been identified, its full-length sequence, ligand specificity, and functional role in human NK cell biology remain unvalidated. Establishing the true functional human homolog is essential for translating murine mechanistic studies on NK cell regulation, immune synapse formation, and lymphoproliferative disorders to human immunology and potential therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, full-length human CD2/SLAM family member exists that is the functional ortholog of mouse 2B4, binding human CD48 and recapitulating its characteristic signaling through SAP adaptor recruitment to modulate NK cell function.
proposals.method: We will first clone candidate human genes using low-stringency hybridization of a human leukocyte cDNA library with the murine 2B4 cytoplasmic tail probe and degenerate PCR targeting conserved CD2/SLAM family extracellular domains. Candidates will be screened via a parallel reconstitution assay: co-transfection with human CD48 and a SAP-GFP reporter into COS-7 cells to identify clones that mediate specific adhesion and SAP recruitment. The lead candidate will be stably expressed in the NK-like cell line YTS (SAP-deficient) or a derived CRISPR-Cas9 CD48 knockout to test its ability to modulate cytotoxicity against CD48+ target cells and immune synapse formation, directly compared to murine 2B4 controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the human 2B4 homolog, providing the molecular toolset to study its role in human NK and T cell biology. It will establish a foundational model for comparing SLAM family receptor function across species and clarify the signaling pathways relevant to X-linked lymphoproliferative disease (XLP), where SAP mutations occur.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sequence-based identification, this study employs an integrated functional screen—binding plus signaling—to define the homolog, ensuring biological equivalence beyond mere sequence similarity. The direct side-by-side comparison of human and mouse receptor function in an isogenic cellular system is a novel and rigorous approach.

proposals.title: Identification and Molecular Cloning of the Human Homolog of the Murine NK Cell Receptor 2B4
proposals.motivation: The murine 2B4 receptor is a critical regulator of NK cell function, mediating both stimulatory and inhibitory signals. Its ligand, CD48, is a member of the immunoglobulin superfamily, and the human LFA-3 is a homolog of mouse CD48. Identifying the human 2B4 homolog is essential for translating murine NK cell biology to human immunology, particularly in understanding NK cell development from marrow progenitors and their role in graft-versus-host responses.
proposals.hypothesis: A human gene with significant sequence homology to the murine 2B4 gene exists, is expressed on human NK cells, and encodes a receptor that interacts with LFA-3 (CD58), the human functional homolog of murine CD48.
proposals.method: We will screen a human NK cell cDNA library using low-stringency hybridization with the full-length murine 2B4 cDNA probe. Positive clones will be sequenced and analyzed for open reading frames and Ig-like domains. Expression will be confirmed via Northern blot on human NK, T, and B cell lines. Transfection of the candidate cDNA into COS-7 cells will be performed to test for surface expression and binding to soluble LFA-3-Fc fusion protein using flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first molecular characterization of the human 2B4 homolog, establishing a direct genetic link between mouse and human NK cell receptor systems. It will enable functional studies on human NK cell activation and inhibition, with implications for understanding NK cell biology in transplantation and immunity.
proposals.novelty: While the murine 2B4 receptor is well-characterized, its human counterpart remains unidentified. This proposal leverages the known homology between mouse CD48 and human LFA-3 to specifically target its putative receptor, a strategy not yet reported in the literature as of 2000.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Mapping of 2B4 Signaling Architecture and Its Quantitative Modulation by Ly49A at the NK Cell Immunological Synapse
proposals.motivation: The 2B4 receptor is a pivotal co-stimulatory molecule in NK cell activation, but its signaling output is complex and can be inhibitory in the absence of the adaptor SAP. How 2B4 signaling complexes are dynamically assembled and how inhibitory receptors like Ly49A quantitatively tune this process at the single-cell level remains unknown. A high-resolution, single-cell analysis is needed to move beyond population averages and understand the heterogeneous signaling logic that determines NK cell functional decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 2B4 initiates heterogeneous signaling complexes at the single-cell level, characterized by distinct kinetic recruitment of SAP, SHIP-1, and Vav1, and that the inhibitory Ly49A receptor quantitatively modulates the composition and timing of these complexes to fine-tune functional output.
proposals.method: We will generate primary murine NK cells from C57BL/6 and SAP-deficient hosts, transduce them with Ly49A and fluorescent FRET-based biosensors for SAP recruitment, SHIP-1 membrane localization, and Vav1 phosphorylation, alongside a cytosolic calcium indicator. Using high-speed, multi-channel live-cell imaging on a spinning-disk confocal microscope, we will analyze single NK cell synapses with RMA-S target cells expressing defined CD48 (2B4 ligand) and H-2Dd (Ly49A ligand). Signaling kinetics and spatial organization will be quantified using custom CellProfiler pipelines and correlated with functional outcomes (calcium flux, degranulation) in the same cell.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first single-cell, high-dimensional map of 2B4 signaling architecture, directly linking molecular recruitment events to functional output. It will define the quantitative rules by which an inhibitory receptor tunes a stimulatory pathway in real time, providing a new framework for understanding NK cell signal integration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, simultaneous visualization of multiple endogenous signaling nodes within a single, living NK cell during synapse formation, enabling the correlation of heterogeneous molecular events with functional fate, a resolution not achieved by prior biochemical or population-based studies.

proposals.title: Generation and Phenotypic Analysis of 2B4-Deficient Mice Reveals a Critical Role in NK Cell Development and Function
proposals.motivation: The 2B4 receptor is a key regulator of natural killer (NK) cell activity, but its precise role in vivo remains ambiguous due to conflicting reports of its function as stimulatory or inhibitory. Pre-2026 studies, including our own characterization of murine 2B4, have relied on antibody blockade or in vitro assays, which cannot fully recapitulate its integrated physiological function. A definitive genetic model is required to dissect the non-redundant role of 2B4 in NK cell development, homeostasis, and anti-tumor or anti-viral responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic ablation of the 2B4 gene will disrupt NK cell development from flt3+ marrow progenitors and impair NK cell functional maturation, leading to specific in vivo deficits in immune surveillance.
proposals.method: We will employ embryonic stem (ES) cell gene targeting in C57BL/6-derived ES cells. A targeting vector will be constructed to replace a critical exon of the Cd244 (2B4) gene with a neomycin resistance cassette via homologous recombination. Correctly targeted ES clones will be identified by Southern blot analysis and used to generate chimeric mice via blastocyst injection into BALB/c hosts. Germline transmission will yield heterozygous mice, which will be intercrossed to produce homozygous 2B4 knockout (2B4-/-) mice. Phenotypic analysis will involve multi-parameter flow cytometry of bone marrow, spleen, and blood to assess NK cell (NK1.1+, Ly49+) development, alongside in vitro functional assays (cytotoxicity, cytokine production) and in vivo challenges with MHC class I-deficient tumor grafts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive genetic model to study 2B4 function in an intact immune system, clarifying its role as a developmental checkpoint or functional modulator. It will establish a foundational resource for the field to investigate 2B4 in infection, cancer, and potentially in the context of hematopoietic cell grafts without confounding antibody effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work used antibodies or focused on the human homolog, no pre-2026 study has reported a germline 2B4 knockout mouse, making this a novel and essential tool to resolve long-standing contradictions in the literature regarding this receptor's biology.

proposals.title: Functional and Clinical Characterization of the Human 2B4 Homolog in Lymphocytes from Patients with Primary Immunodeficiencies and Hematopoietic Graft Complications
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including by Kumar et al., has established murine 2B4 as a critical co-receptor on NK cells and T cell subsets with dual inhibitory and activating functions, influencing cytotoxicity and cytokine production. The human homolog has been molecularly identified, but its precise functional roles in human immune disorders and its potential involvement in the pathophysiology of conditions like graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) or post-hematopoietic cell transplant reconstitution remain poorly defined. Translating murine findings to human clinical contexts is a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: The human 2B4 receptor exhibits dysregulated expression and/or signaling in lymphocytes from patients with specific primary immunodeficiencies or post-transplant complications, and this dysregulation directly contributes to aberrant immune cell function and disease pathogenesis.
proposals.method: We will isolate peripheral blood lymphocytes from consented patient cohorts (e.g., with familial hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (FHL) variants, post-allogeneic marrow transplant patients with/without GVHD) and healthy BALB/c and humanized mouse model controls. Using multi-parameter flow cytometry, we will quantify 2B4 surface expression on NK, CD8+ T, and γδ T cell subsets. Functional assays will include redirected lysis using P815 target cells coated with anti-2B4 antibodies and phospho-flow cytometry to assess downstream SAP/SHIP-1 signaling pathways upon 2B4 engagement. Correlative analysis will link receptor phenotype to clinical disease severity and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive functional profile of human 2B4 in clinically relevant patient samples, directly linking its biology to human disease.
