It will establish whether 2B4 serves as a potential biomarker for immune dysregulation and offer mechanistic insights that could inform future immunomodulatory therapies targeting this pathway in transplant and hyperinflammatory settings.
proposals.novelty: While the human 2B4 gene is known, this proposal moves beyond basic molecular characterization to a direct, systematic investigation of its protein-level function in primary human patient lymphocytes, specifically within the understudied context of hematopoietic grafts and related immunodeficiencies, bridging mouse genetics and human clinical immunology.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of the Cellular Ligand(s) for the NK Cell Receptor 2B4
proposals.motivation: The 2B4 receptor (CD244) is a critical regulator of NK cell function, transmitting both inhibitory and activating signals. While 2B4's role in NK cell biology and its interaction with CD48 in mice is established, its human ligand(s) remain incompletely defined, with CD48 being a low-affinity binder. Identifying the primary cellular ligand(s) for human 2B4 is essential to fully understand its physiological role in immune surveillance, particularly against hematopoietic malignancies and in the context of bone marrow transplantation where NK cell activity is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary functional ligand for human 2B4 is expressed on specific hematopoietic cell populations, including certain leukemias and antigen-presenting cells, and that its engagement modulates NK cell cytotoxicity and cytokine production.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach: 1) A ligand identification screen using a recombinant 2B4-Fc fusion protein to stain a panel of murine (Balb/c-derived) and human hematopoietic cell lines, primary leukocytes, and FLT3+ marrow progenitor cell grafts. 2) Functional validation using primary human NK cells as effectors in cytotoxicity and IFN-γ release assays against ligand-positive target cells, with blockade using anti-2B4 monoclonal antibodies. Receptor-ligand interaction will be further confirmed by co-capping experiments and flow cytometric binding analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the cellular ligand for human 2B4, resolving a key gap in NK cell biology. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how 2B4 signaling is regulated in trans, with implications for understanding NK cell tolerance in marrow grafts and their anti-leukemic potential.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kumar et al. focused on characterizing the 2B4 receptor itself, this proposal directly addresses the inverse, unsolved problem: the identity and distribution of its cognate cellular ligand, moving from receptor biochemistry to functional cellular immunology.

proposals.title: Functional and Transcriptomic Plasticity of Murine Natural Killer Cells: NK-A and NK-B as Activation States of a Single Lineage
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, murine NK cells are functionally subdivided into NK-A (CD27+ CD11b-) and NK-B (CD27- CD11b+) subsets, often considered distinct lineages with separate developmental pathways. This paradigm influences models of NK cell development, differentiation, and function. However, the possibility that these subsets represent activation or maturation states of a single lineage, influenced by microenvironmental cues, remains underexplored and could fundamentally reshape our understanding of NK cell biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that murine NK-A and NK-B cells are not separate lineages but represent different activation or tissue-adaptation states of a single NK cell lineage, with interconversion driven by cytokine signals and tissue-specific environmental factors.
proposals.method: We will use congenic (CD45.1/45.2) BALB/c mice and adoptive transfer models. NK-A cells (sorted as Lin- NK1.1+ CD27+ CD11b-) from donor marrow or spleen will be transferred into sublethally irradiated or immunodeficient (e.g., Rag2-/- γc-/-) hosts. Recipients will be analyzed by flow cytometry at serial time points for conversion to NK-B phenotype (CD27- CD11b+). We will employ in vivo cytokine blockade (anti-IL-15/IL-15Rα) and use Flt3L-expanded marrow progenitor grafts to test developmental plasticity. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics) of donor-derived cells pre- and post-transfer will define transcriptional trajectories and identify key regulators of state transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive experimental evidence for or against the lineage vs. state model for major NK cell subsets. It will establish a new framework for NK cell differentiation based on functional plasticity rather than rigid lineage commitment. The findings will directly impact the interpretation of NK cell responses in infection, cancer, and bone marrow transplantation contexts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kumar et al. and others characterized NK receptors and progenitor potential, directly testing the interconversion of NK-A and NK-B subsets in vivo using adoptive transfer and scRNA-seq is novel. It challenges a core classification in the field by applying a dynamic, state-based perspective informed by modern single-cell analysis.

proposals.title: Dissecting the beige mutation's impact: A developmental and functional analysis of NK cells in C57BL/6-bg/bg mice
proposals.motivation: The beige (bg/bg) mutation in mice, a model for human Chediak-Higashi syndrome, is historically linked to severe NK cell cytotoxicity defects. However, the precise cellular basis—whether it is a developmental defect in the bone marrow, a failure of mature cell maintenance, or a purely functional impairment in cytotoxicity—remains ambiguous. Clarifying this is crucial for understanding the pathophysiology of NK cell deficiencies and for interpreting data from this widely used model.
proposals.hypothesis: The bg/bg mutation primarily impairs the cytotoxic function of mature NK cells, including granule exocytosis and target cell lysis, without fundamentally disrupting their development from flt3+ marrow progenitors, their marrow dependence, or their surface receptor expression profile.
proposals.method: We will use congenic C57BL/6 (wild-type) and C57BL/6-bg/bg mice. First, we will perform detailed flow cytometric analysis of bone marrow and spleen using panels for NK cell developmental stages (e.g., NK1.1, CD122, CD27, CD11b, Ly49 receptors) and receptor expression (including 2B4). Second, we will assess in vivo marrow dependence by treating bg/bg mice with 5-fluorouracil to ablate proliferating progenitors and track NK cell reconstitution. Third, we will conduct ex vivo cytotoxicity assays against YAC-1 and RMA-S targets, and measure perforin/granzyme B content and mobilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively separate the developmental and functional consequences of the beige mutation, providing a clearer mechanistic understanding of the NK cell defect. It will refine the use of the bg/bg mouse model for studies of cytotoxicity pathways versus developmental biology and inform potential therapeutic strategies for Chediak-Higashi syndrome.
proposals.novelty: While the bg/bg phenotype is well-known, a systematic analysis dissecting development, marrow dependence, and receptor expression from terminal effector function has not been comprehensively performed, especially in the context of modern NK cell developmental markers and receptor biology.

proposals.title: Systematic Assessment of 89Sr-Induced Marrow Ablation on Extramedullary NK Cell Niches and Function
proposals.motivation: The radiopharmaceutical 89Sr is a standard tool for selective ablation of bone marrow in murine models, enabling studies of hematopoietic reconstitution and NK cell development from marrow progenitors. However, its potential off-target effects on other critical NK cell niches, such as the spleen, liver, and lymph nodes, are poorly characterized. Understanding the specificity of 89Sr is crucial for interpreting data from ablation-reconstitution studies, especially regarding the role of marrow-derived vs. tissue-resident NK cell precursors and mature subsets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while 89Sr effectively ablates the bone marrow compartment, it spares key extramedullary niches, preserving the development and function of NK cells that are independent of ongoing marrow output in the short term.
proposals.method: We will treat BALB/c mice with 89Sr and analyze tissues at multiple timepoints. Using flow cytometry, we will quantify NK cell progenitors (e.g., flt3+ marrow progenitors) and mature NK cells (NK1.1+, Ly49+) in bone marrow, spleen, liver, and blood. We will assess NK cell function via in vitro cytotoxicity assays against YAC-1 targets and measure receptor expression profiles (e.g., 2B4). To test niche independence, we will perform adoptive transfers of splenocytes from 89Sr-treated mice into NK-deficient hosts and evaluate reconstitution capacity compared to controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the tissue-specific impact of 89Sr, clarifying its utility and limitations for studying NK cell biology. It will provide new insights into the resilience of extramedullary NK cell pools, informing models of NK cell development and homeostasis. The data will be critical for the design and interpretation of future graft and progenitor studies.
proposals.novelty: While 89Sr is widely used, a systematic, multi-tissue analysis of its effects specifically on NK cell compartments is lacking. This study uniquely integrates assessment of progenitor ablation, mature niche persistence, and functional competence across all major NK cell sites.

proposals.title: Partial Donor Chimerism is Sufficient for Potent Graft-versus-Leukemia Effects Mediated by Donor-Derived NK Cells in a Murine Model
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT) aims to cure hematologic malignancies via a graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect, but is limited by the morbidity of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) and the toxicity of conditioning regimens required for full donor chimerism. A major pre-2026 clinical question is whether reduced-intensity conditioning, which often results in mixed chimerism, can provide sufficient anti-leukemic immunity. This proposal directly tests the chimerism requirement for GVL, a key parameter for optimizing transplant strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a potent GVL effect, mediated primarily by donor-derived natural killer (NK) cells, does not require full donor hematopoietic chimerism and can be achieved in a state of stable mixed chimerism.
proposals.method: We will use a established murine BALB/c (H-2d) to C57BL/6 (H-2b) transplantation model. Lethally irradiated B6 hosts will receive T-cell-depleted bone marrow from BALB/c donors to establish full chimerism controls. Sub-lethally irradiated hosts will receive graded doses of BALB/c marrow to establish stable mixed chimerism, quantified by flow cytometry for H-2Dd and H-2Db. All groups will be challenged with a lethal dose of A20 leukemia cells (BALB/c origin, H-2d). GVL will be assessed by survival and tumor burden. The role of donor NK cells will be evaluated using NK1.1 depletion and by analyzing NK cell receptor (e.g., Ly49, 2B4) repertoire and function in mixed vs. full chimeras.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the minimum level of donor chimerism required for effective NK-mediated GVL, providing a preclinical rationale for less toxic, reduced-intensity transplant regimens. It will also elucidate how the hematopoietic environment of mixed chimerism shapes the reconstitution and anti-tumor function of donor NK cells, linking chimerism state to immune effector quality.
proposals.novelty: While mixed chimerism has been studied for tolerance induction, its sufficiency for robust GVL, specifically dissecting the NK cell contribution, is not well-defined. This directly tests the paradigm that complete host replacement is necessary for curative anti-cancer immunity.

proposals.title: Selective Amplification of Donor-Derived NK Cells via 2B4 and SLAM Receptor Modulation to Augment Graft-versus-Leukemia While Mitigating Graft-versus-Host Disease in Murine Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT) is a curative therapy for leukemia, but its efficacy is limited by the tight linkage between the beneficial graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect and the detrimental graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). Natural killer (NK) cells, derived from donor hematopoietic grafts, can mediate potent GVL without causing classical GVHD, making them ideal therapeutic effectors. However, current strategies to expand NK cells in vivo post-transplant are non-selective and risk amplifying alloreactive T cells that drive GVHD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ex vivo modulation of the signaling balance in donor-derived NK cell progenitors, specifically by enhancing stimulatory 2B4 and SLAM family receptor signaling, will selectively amplify a functional, mature NK cell compartment in vivo, leading to enhanced GVL without exacerbating GVHD in a murine allo-HCT model.
proposals.method: We will use the established BALB/c→C57BL/6 (H-2d→H-2b) murine model of MHC-mismatched allo-HCT. Donor bone marrow (flt3+ multipotent progenitor-enriched) and splenic T cells will be transplanted into lethally irradiated hosts. Prior to transplant, donor marrow progenitor cells will be transduced with a lentiviral vector expressing a chimeric receptor where the extracellular domain of 2B4 is fused to the intracellular signaling domain of SLAM-associated protein (SAP), designed to convert 2B4 interactions into constitutive SAP-mediated stimulatory signals. GVL will be assessed by co-injecting A20 leukemia/lymphoma cells (BALB/c origin), and GVHD will be monitored clinically and histologically. The differentiation, expansion, and receptor repertoire (NK1.1, Ly49) of donor-derived NK cells will be tracked weekly using flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel progenitor cell engineering strategy to selectively bias post-transplant immune reconstitution towards the NK cell lineage. It will provide direct pre-clinical evidence that amplifying NK cells via specific receptor signaling pathways can uncouple GVL from GVHD. The findings will inform the development of next-generation 'smart' grafts for allo-HCT.
proposals.novelty: While NK cell infusion is known, this approach uniquely engineers the graft's *progenitor cells* to autonomously generate an amplified NK compartment *in vivo*. It leverages Dr. Kumar's expertise in 2B4/SLAM biology and NK development to create a sustained, graft-intrinsic GVL effect, which is distinct from bolus NK cell therapies.

proposals.title: Mechanisms of Graft-versus-Tumor Activity in a Murine Renal Carcinoma Model Following Nonmyeloablative Allogeneic Bone Marrow Transplantation
proposals.motivation: Nonmyeloablative stem cell transplantation (NMSCT) has shown promise in hematologic malignancies, but its application to solid tumors like renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is limited. Pre-2026 clinical observations suggest rare but potent antitumor responses in RCC patients after allogeneic transplantation, implying a graft-versus-tumor (GVT) effect, yet the underlying cellular and molecular mechanisms remain poorly defined. This gap hinders the rational development of NMSCT as immunotherapy for solid tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NMSCT in a murine RCC model induces a donor-derived NK cell-mediated GVT response, and that this response is critically regulated by the balance of signaling through the 2B4 receptor and other Ly49 family receptors.
proposals.method: We will establish a murine RENCA renal carcinoma model in BALB/c (H-2d) mice. Recipients will undergo nonmyeloablative conditioning (low-dose irradiation) followed by transplantation of T-cell-depleted bone marrow from allogeneic C57BL/6 (H-2b) donors. Tumor growth and survival will be monitored. Using flow cytometry, we will track the differentiation and expansion of donor-derived NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells from Flt3+ progenitor cells within the graft. The functional role of these NK cells and the 2B4 receptor will be assessed using in vivo depletion (anti-NK1.1) and blockade (anti-2B4 monoclonal antibodies, comparing inhibitory vs. stimulatory forms) to quantify their contribution to tumor control and graft-versus-host disease.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive preclinical model for NMSCT in solid tumors, directly testing the cellular basis of the GVT effect. It will elucidate the specific NK cell differentiation pathway and receptor-ligand interactions (particularly 2B4) required for antitumor efficacy, offering mechanistic insights to guide patient selection and adjunctive therapies in future clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kumar's prior work defined 2B4 biology and NK development in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates these concepts to mechanistically dissect NMSCT for a solid tumor, a context where the role of developing allogeneic NK cells is unknown.

proposals.title: A Murine Model for Controlling Graft-versus-Host Disease via a 2B4-Targeted Inducible Caspase 9 Suicide Gene in Donor Lymphocytes
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation is limited by graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), where donor T cells attack host tissues. While suicide gene therapy using inducible caspase 9 (iCasp9) has shown promise in early-phase human trials for controlling engineered T cells, its application to prevent GVHD from unmodified donor lymphocyte infusions (DLI) or marrow grafts remains underexplored in pre-clinical models. A major challenge is identifying a safe and efficient target for delivering the suicide gene specifically to GVHD-causing lymphocytes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering donor bone marrow progenitor cells to express an iCasp9 suicide gene under the control of the *Nkrb1c* (2B4) receptor promoter will generate a graft in which NK and T cell progeny can be selectively ablated upon administration of a chemical inducer of dimerization (AP1903), thereby mitigating GVHD while preserving other hematopoietic lineages.
proposals.method: We will use a retroviral vector to express iCasp9 and a fluorescent reporter under the murine 2B4 promoter/enhancer elements. BALB/c (H-2d) donor lineage-negative, flt3+ marrow progenitor cells will be transduced and transplanted into lethally irradiated C57BL/6 (H-2b) hosts. After engraftment, we will assess baseline reconstitution of NK1.1+, Ly49+ NK cells and T cells. To model GVHD control, we will administer AP1903 and quantify ablation of 2B4+ lymphocytes by flow cytometry and monitor mice for clinical and histological signs of GVHD compared to untreated controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel pre-clinical platform for suicide gene therapy in allogeneic transplantation, directly testing the 2B4 promoter for lineage-specific suicide in vivo. It will provide critical proof-of-concept data on whether targeting a receptor central to NK cell biology (2B4) can effectively control lymphocyte populations to improve transplant safety, informing future translational strategies.
proposals.novelty: While iCasp9 systems exist, their coupling with the 2B4 promoter to target donor-derived lymphoid progeny from multipotent grafts is novel. This approach leverages Dr. Kumar's expertise in 2B4 biology and NK cell development to create a more physiologically relevant and controllable safety switch than broadly expressed viral promoters.

proposals.title: Harnessing Residual Host NK Cells for a Host-versus-Graft Leukemia Effect Following Reduced-Intensity Conditioning and Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation
proposals.motivation: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) regimens for allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT) reduce toxicity but are associated with higher rates of leukemia relapse. A key factor is the incomplete eradication of host immunity, which can lead to graft rejection but may also contain anti-leukemic effectors. This proposal seeks to invert the traditional paradigm by investigating whether these residual host immune cells, particularly Natural Killer (NK) cells, can be therapeutically directed against residual leukemia, creating a beneficial 'host-versus-graft leukemia' (HvGL) effect prior to full donor engraftment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that host NK cells, persisting after RIC, can be functionally augmented via cytokine administration and receptor modulation to specifically recognize and eliminate residual leukemia cells, thereby reducing early relapse rates in a murine model of allo-HCT.
proposals.method: We will use a established murine model (BALB/c hosts, C57BL/6 donors) with a syngeneic leukemia (e.g., C1498). Mice will undergo RIC followed by T-cell-depleted allogeneic bone marrow grafts. Experimental groups will receive post-transplant IL-15/IL-15Ra complex to expand host NK cells, and blocking antibodies against the inhibitory receptor 2B4 (CD244) to modulate its function, based on our prior characterization of this pathway. Leukemia burden, donor/host chimerism, and the phenotype (NK1.1, Ly49 subsets) and function (cytotoxicity, IFN-γ production) of persisting host versus donor NK cells will be longitudinally analyzed by flow cytometry and in vivo bioluminescence imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for the feasibility of a therapeutically induced HvGL effect. It will delineate the functional capacity of host NK cells in the post-RIC window and identify specific molecular targets, like 2B4, for enhancing their anti-leukemic activity. This could lead to novel adjuvant therapies that leverage the host immune system to bridge the critical period until donor immunity is fully established.
proposals.novelty: While all research focuses on maximizing the graft-versus-leukemia effect, this proposal uniquely investigates harnessing the often-suppressed host immunity as an additional anti-leukemic force. The approach combines the clinical context of RIC with precise immunomodulation of NK cell receptors, specifically 2B4, which we have previously characterized in inhibitory and stimulatory contexts.

proposals.title: A Synthetic Biology Approach to NK Cell Discrimination: Replacing Variegated Inhibitory Receptor Networks with a Uniform Engineered Signal
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell self-tolerance and discrimination are governed by a complex, variegated mosaic of inhibitory receptors (e.g., Ly49, KIR) with distinct specificities and expression patterns across subsets. While this system is effective, its necessity for precise discrimination is unknown. This complexity is a barrier to engineering predictable NK cell therapies. We ask if this native 'circuit' can be simplified, akin to synthetic biology replacements of natural gene networks, to achieve core function.
proposals.hypothesis: The nuanced self/non-self discrimination of NK cells is an emergent property of integrated signaling thresholds that can be replicated by a single, uniformly expressed engineered inhibitory receptor providing a fixed-strength signal, independent of natural ligand-binding diversity.
proposals.method: We will engineer a 'universal inhibitory receptor' (UIR) by fusing the extracellular domain of a non-mammalian protein (e.g., GFP) to the transmembrane and intracellular domains of a potent inhibitory receptor like murine 2B4 or Ly49A. This UIR will be constitutively expressed via retroviral transduction in primary flt3+ murine marrow progenitor cells or NK cells from Balb/c mice. A soluble, high-affinity ligand (e.g., anti-GFP nanobody-Fc fusion) will be coated on various target cells (e.g., YAC-1, RMA-S, MHC-I-deficient hosts, and healthy syngeneic cells) to provide a uniform inhibitory signal. NK cell function (cytotoxicity, IFN-γ production) against these targets will be compared to wild-type NK cells with natural receptor variegation.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a fundamental principle of NK cell biology: whether complex inhibitory receptor diversity is necessary for discrimination. It will establish a proof-of-concept for radically simplifying the NK cell inhibitory axis, providing a new framework for engineering NK cells with more predictable 'off-the-shelf' therapeutic profiles, decoupled from host MHC.
proposals.novelty: While NK cell engineering focuses on adding activating receptors, systematically replacing the entire native inhibitory system with a single, orthogonal, uniform signal is unprecedented. It applies a core synthetic biology principle—network simplification and modularization—to a primary immune cell discrimination system.

proposals.title: Engineering a Gain-of-Function Ly49A Receptor to Convert NK Cell Inhibition to Activation and Potentiate Marrow Graft Rejection
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell education and function are critically regulated by the balance of inhibitory and activating receptors, such as the Ly49 family in mice. The inhibitory Ly49A receptor, specific for H-2D^d, dampens NK cell responses, which can be detrimental in settings like hematopoietic stem cell transplantation where robust NK activity against residual host or malignant cells is desired. Pre-2026 work has characterized inhibitory signaling via immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibitory motifs (ITIMs) in Ly49A and activating isoforms in receptors like 2B4, but directly reprogramming a canonical inhibitory Ly49 receptor to an activator in vivo remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the cytoplasmic tail of the inhibitory Ly49A receptor with a canonical activating signaling domain (e.g., from DAP12 or FcRγ) will create a gain-of-function receptor that converts MHC-I recognition into an activating signal, thereby enhancing the cytotoxic potential of NK cells and accelerating the rejection of MHC-mismatched bone marrow grafts.
proposals.method: We will engineer a chimeric Ly49A receptor by fusing its extracellular and transmembrane domains to the cytoplasmic tail of the DAP12 adapter protein, which contains an immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motif (ITAM). This construct will be cloned into a retroviral vector for transduction into primary flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells from C57BL/6 mice (H-2^b). These transduced progenitors will be differentiated into NK cells (NK1.1+, Ly49+) in vitro. The function of engineered NK cells will be tested in vivo using a BALB/c (H-2^d) marrow graft rejection model in irradiated C57BL/6 hosts, comparing rejection kinetics to hosts reconstituted with control NK cells expressing wild-type Ly49A.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for functionally inverting a major inhibitory NK cell receptor lineage, providing a novel tool to study NK cell education and signal integration. It will directly test the hypothesis that Ly49A-mediated recognition can be harnessed to drive, rather than suppress, graft rejection, offering a potential blueprint for engineering NK cells for enhanced immunotherapeutic function in transplantation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have characterized natural or engineered activating NK receptors (e.g., 2B4), this proposal is novel in deliberately re-engineering a prototypical inhibitory Ly49 receptor—central to NK self-tolerance—into a potent activator, and testing its impact in a physiologically relevant transplantation model.

proposals.title: Competitive fitness of NK cells with uniform versus variegated Ly49A expression reveals a functional cost in multi-challenge surveillance
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cells exhibit variegated, subset-specific expression of inhibitory Ly49 receptors for self-MHC-I, a phenomenon whose functional rationale remains incompletely understood. A leading hypothesis is that this complex receptor distribution optimizes the NK cell repertoire for discrimination in diverse challenges, but a direct test of the functional cost of an alternative, uniform expression strategy has been lacking. This gap limits our understanding of the evolutionary pressures shaping the NK cell receptor repertoire.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that uniform expression of the inhibitory receptor Ly49A across all NK cells imposes a net fitness cost compared to the natural variegated pattern when cells must respond to combined physiological challenges, such as tumor surveillance and viral infection, within a single host.
proposals.method: We will generate two isogenic NK cell populations from flt3+ multipotent marrow progenitor cells of C57BL/6 mice, using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing to enforce uniform Ly49A expression in one and to recapitulate normal variegation in the other, with each population marked by distinct congenic CD45 alleles. These populations will be mixed at a 1:1 ratio and adoptively transferred into sub-lethally irradiated BALB/c hosts (lacking the Ly49A ligand H-2Dd), which will then be subjected to a dual challenge with B16 melanoma cells and murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV). The relative expansion and functional state (via intracellular IFN-γ and granzyme B staining) of each congenic population in blood, spleen, and tumor will be tracked weekly by flow cytometry to calculate a competitive fitness index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measure of the functional cost of a uniform inhibitory receptor strategy in NK cells within a physiologically complex environment. It will empirically test the long-standing theoretical framework that receptor variegation is essential for optimal multi-task discrimination, thereby linking NK cell developmental biology to functional immunology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting a classical competitive fitness assay from evolutionary biology to directly test the functional value of an immune receptor expression pattern, and in using a combined tumor-viral challenge to simulate the multi-faceted environment that likely shaped NK cell repertoire selection.

proposals.title: Forced pan-NK cell expression of Ly49A impairs NK cell repertoire diversity and anti-tumor surveillance in vivo
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cell education and function depend on a diverse repertoire of inhibitory Ly49 receptors for self-MHC-I recognition. The functional significance of the variegated, subset-restricted expression pattern of Ly49 receptors, however, remains unclear. Determining whether this stochastic expression is necessary for optimal NK cell discrimination between healthy and aberrant cells, particularly in tumor surveillance, is a fundamental question in NK cell biology with implications for immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enforcing uniform Ly49A expression across all NK cells will disrupt the natural repertoire, leading to improper education, reduced functional diversity, and impaired discrimination, thereby compromising anti-tumor responses in vivo.
proposals.method: We will generate a novel transgenic mouse model (C57BL/6 background) where the Ly49A coding sequence is placed under the control of the pan-NK cell-specific Ncr1 (NKp46) promoter. Bone marrow from these mice will be used to reconstitute irradiated Balb/c hosts, creating a scenario where all developing NK cells uniformly express Ly49A (specific for H-2D^d) in an H-2^b→H-2^d bone marrow chimera. We will analyze NK cell education, repertoire (using Ly49-specific antibodies), and functional capacity against MHC-I-deficient (RMA-S) and MHC-I-sufficient tumor targets in vitro. In vivo tumor surveillance will be assessed by challenging mice with B16F10 melanoma and RMA-S lymphoma.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the functional importance of stochastic Ly49 receptor expression, providing the first in vivo evidence that repertoire diversity is necessary for effective NK cell discrimination and tumor immune surveillance. It will establish a novel transgenic model for studying NK cell education and could inform the design of NK cell-based therapies by highlighting the risks of perturbing natural receptor distributions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterized Ly49 receptors individually, no study has artificially homogenized the expression of a single inhibitory receptor across the entire NK cell compartment to test the functional necessity of variegation for systemic immune surveillance.

proposals.title: In vivo blockade of the inhibitory receptor Ly49A disrupts NK cell tolerance and accelerates allogeneic bone marrow graft rejection in mice
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cells express inhibitory receptors, like Ly49A in mice, which recognize self-MHC class I and prevent autoimmunity. In transplantation, recipient NK cells can reject MHC-mismatched grafts, but the specific role of blocking these inhibitory pathways to induce rejection is not fully defined. This project translates the concept of antibody-mediated checkpoint blockade, used in autoimmunity to activate immune cells, to the transplantation setting to test if disrupting NK cell inhibition is sufficient to trigger graft rejection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo monoclonal antibody blockade of the inhibitory NK cell receptor Ly49A will release NK cells from inhibition, enhancing their activation against allogeneic cells and leading to the rejection of MHC-mismatched bone marrow grafts.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established murine bone marrow transplantation model. BALB/c (H-2d) hosts will be irradiated and reconstituted with fully allogeneic C57BL/6 (H-2b) bone marrow. Experimental groups will receive intraperitoneal injections of a function-blocking monoclonal antibody against Ly49A (e.g., clone A1, or similar), while controls receive isotype antibody. Graft rejection will be assessed by peripheral blood chimerism analysis via flow cytometry for donor (H-2b) and host (H-2d) markers at weekly intervals. NK cell activation status (CD69, IFN-γ) and cytotoxicity in vivo will be concurrently evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the sufficiency of a single inhibitory receptor blockade in triggering transplant rejection, moving beyond correlative studies. It will establish a proof-of-concept for targeting NK cell inhibitory checkpoints as a strategy to modulate transplant outcomes, potentially informing approaches to purge residual malignant cells in allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation.
proposals.novelty: While Ly49A's role in self-tolerance is known, its targeted blockade to deliberately induce allograft rejection is a novel therapeutic inversion of autoimmunity principles. This approach is distinct from prior work characterizing 2B4 or other receptors, focusing on a definitive in vivo functional outcome in transplantation.

proposals.title: Adoptive Transfer of Defined NK Cell Subsets Reveals a Critical Role for Ly49A+ NK Cells in Allogeneic Marrow Graft Rejection
proposals.motivation: Natural killer (NK) cells are potent mediators of bone marrow graft rejection, particularly in MHC-mismatched transplants. However, the specific contributions of distinct NK cell subsets, defined by inhibitory receptor expression, remain poorly dissected. A functional understanding of which NK subsets drive rejection is crucial for developing targeted immunomodulatory strategies to improve transplant outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Ly49A+ NK cell subset, which recognizes H-2D^d, is the primary effector population responsible for rejecting allogeneic (BALB/c, H-2^d) bone marrow grafts in C57BL/6 (H-2^b) hosts, and that this activity is regulated by the balance of signals through receptors like 2B4.
proposals.method: NK cell subsets (Ly49A+ vs. Ly49A-) will be FACS-sorted from C57BL/6 donor mice based on NK1.1 and Ly49A expression. These defined populations will be adoptively transferred into NK-deficient (e.g., Il2rg^-/-) or Rag2^-/-Il2rg^-/- DKO hosts. Recipient mice will then be challenged with CFSE-labeled BALB/c (allogeneic) or C57BL/6 (syngeneic) bone marrow cells. Graft rejection will be quantified by flow cytometric analysis of donor-derived cells in host marrow and spleen 5-7 days post-transfer. The role of 2B4 will be probed using blocking antibodies or cells from 2B4-deficient mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly identify the NK cell subset most responsible for allogeneic marrow rejection, moving beyond bulk NK cell analyses. It will clarify the functional interplay between Ly49 receptors and co-stimulatory molecules like 2B4 in a definitive in vivo rejection model. The findings will provide a mechanistic framework for subset-specific NK cell targeting in transplantation.
proposals.novelty: While NK cells are known to reject grafts, the adoptive transfer of precisely defined, sorted NK subsets into immunodeficient hosts to isolate their specific rejection capacity is a direct and granular approach not commonly reported in pre-2026 literature. Combining this with concurrent manipulation of the 2B4 pathway integrates receptor signaling biology with subset-specific functional analysis.

proposals.title: Systematic Screening of Human Leukemia Cell Lines for NK Cell Lysis Patterns Revealing Putative Human Hh-like Antigens
proposals.motivation: In murine models, the Hh (Hemagglutinin-haemagglutinin) antigen system, recognized by the NK cell receptor 2B4, defines a critical missing-self target on hematopoietic cells. The human orthologue of 2B4 has been identified, but its functional ligands on human malignancies, particularly leukemias, remain poorly characterized. Identifying human Hh-like targets could reveal novel pathways for immune evasion and provide targets for NK cell-based immunotherapies in leukemia.
proposals.hypothesis: Human leukemia cell lines exhibit heterogeneous susceptibility to lysis by primary human NK cells, and specific patterns of resistance correlate with the expression of putative human ligands for the 2B4 receptor, functionally analogous to murine Hh antigens.
proposals.method: We will screen a panel of 20-30 well-characterized human leukemia cell lines (e.g., K562, HL-60, THP-1, Jurkat) for lysis by primary human NK cells from healthy donors using standard 4-hour 51Cr-release assays. Patterns of susceptibility will be analyzed using hierarchical clustering. Cell lines showing consistent resistance will be co-cultured with NK cells in antibody-blocking experiments using anti-2B4 (CD244) monoclonal antibodies. Receptor-ligand interaction will be further investigated via protein pull-down assays from resistant cell line lysates using recombinant 2B4-Fc fusion protein, followed by mass spectrometry to identify candidate Hh-like ligands.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first functional map linking human leukemia cell phenotypes to NK cell lysis via the 2B4 pathway. It will identify candidate human Hh-like antigens, providing a direct translational bridge from murine NK cell studies to human leukemia biology. The findings could stratify leukemias based on innate immune susceptibility and inform the design of adoptive NK cell therapies.
proposals.novelty: While murine Hh antigens are defined, this proposal directly tests for their functional equivalents in human leukemias, a scale-up from model systems to human pathology. The approach combines functional NK cell screens with biochemical identification of ligands for a specific, clinically relevant NK receptor (2B4).

ideas.title: Testing a Universal Inhibitory Signal Against a Context-Dependent Natural System
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the natural, variegated expression of inhibitory receptors across NK cell subsets is necessary for their discriminatory function. Drawing an analogy from synthetic biology, where complex native regulatory networks are often replaced with simplified, uniform synthetic circuits, we adapt that solution. We propose to engineer a 'universal' inhibitory signal in NK cells—a single, constitutively expressed engineered receptor with fixed signaling strength—to see if it can fully replicate the natural system's ability to distinguish self from non-self. The adapted research idea involves creating NK cells expressing this uniform inhibitory receptor, decoupled from natural ligand-binding diversity, and rigorously testing their functional output against a wide array of target cells. This directly tests whether the nuanced discrimination of natural NK cells emerges from the specific subset expression pattern or can be achieved with a simplified, uniform inhibitory input.

ideas.title: Assess functional cost of uniform inhibitory receptor expression via competitive fitness assay
ideas.description: This idea adapts the competitive fitness assay from evolutionary biology to immunology. The analogous problem is determining the net cost or benefit of a specific trait in a complex, multi-faceted environment, which is classically solved by co-housing isogenic strains differing only in that trait and measuring their relative success. Here, we adapt this by generating two NK cell populations differing only in Ly49A expression patterns (uniform vs. variegated) and introducing them into a mouse model. The mouse is then subjected to a combined physiological challenge, such as tumor implantation and viral infection, to simulate the multi-task environment of real immune surveillance. By tracking the relative expansion and functional markers of each NK population over time, the assay will reveal whether the 'uniform expression' trait imposes a net fitness cost, thereby determining if subset-specific expression is necessary for optimal, multi-functional discrimination.

ideas.title: Multi-modal single-cell analysis of 2B4 expression dynamics and functional consequence across immune cell states
ideas.description: This study moves beyond bulk population analysis to define the precise regulatory landscape of 2B4 (CD244) at single-cell resolution. Using a combination of ex vivo stimulation, cytokine polarization, and in vitro differentiation models, we will track 2B4 protein expression, transcript levels, and surface density across defined NK and T cell subsets via high-parameter flow cytometry and scRNA-seq. The key innovation is the direct functional coupling of these expression profiles to cellular effector responses, determining whether any observed dynamic regulation translates to altered signaling threshold, cytotoxicity, or cytokine production. This approach will reveal if 2B4 expression is truly constitutive or is finely tuned in specific immunological contexts to modulate lymphocyte function.

ideas.description: This project combines the molecular mapping of Idea A with the single-cell resolution of Idea B to create a high-content, single-cell analysis platform. We will engineer Ly49A-transgenic NK cells with fluorescent biosensors for key 2B4 pathway components (e.g., SAP, SHIP-1, Vav1 phosphorylation) and functional readouts like calcium flux. By imaging single NK cell synapses with target cells presenting defined ligands, we can directly correlate the precise recruitment and activation kinetics of specific 2B4 signaling molecules with the resulting functional response in the same cell. This approach will uncover the heterogeneous 'signaling architectures' downstream of 2B4 and determine how the inhibitory Ly49A receptor quantitatively tunes these 2B4 signaling complexes in real time.

ideas.description: This project first uses low-stringency hybridization and degenerate PCR to clone all potential human CD2/SLAM family members related to mouse 2B4. Candidates are then screened in a parallel assay for their ability to bind human CD48 and initiate intracellular signaling characteristic of SLAM family receptors (e.g., SAP recruitment). The lead candidate is introduced into a suitable null immune cell line to test its capacity to modulate cytotoxicity and immune synapse formation, directly comparing its functional output to that of mouse 2B4. This integrated approach guarantees the identification of the human gene that performs the equivalent biological role, providing a robust foundation for subsequent immunological research.

ideas.title: Quantitative mapping of N-glycosylation site contributions to 2B4 expression, ligand binding, and signaling in primary NK cells
ideas.description: This study systematically dissects the role of each predicted N-glycosylation site in 2B4 function using a comprehensive panel of site-directed mutants. A lentiviral library of 2B4 variants, covering all possible combinations of the eight N-glycan site knockouts, is expressed in primary murine or human NK cells from 2B4-deficient backgrounds. High-resolution quantitative assays measure the impact of each glycan configuration on protein folding (via intracellular staining), surface stability, binding affinity and kinetics for CD48, and downstream signaling outputs. This approach reveals not just necessity, but the quantitative contribution of specific glycosylation sites to discrete stages of 2B4 function in a physiologically relevant cellular context.

ideas.title: Multiplexed, high-resolution FRET biosensor imaging to dissect the causal signaling cascade from single NK cell receptor engagement
ideas.description: This redesigned idea uses primary NK cells from Ly49A-transgenic mice engineered to express a suite of FRET-based biosensors for key signaling molecules (e.g., Src-family kinases, PLCγ, calcium). Upon encounter with ligand-presenting target cells in a microfluidic imaging chamber, the system captures the real-time, sub-second activation kinetics of multiple proximal signaling nodes simultaneously within single cells. This approach directly establishes the causal sequence of the Ly49A-initiated signaling cascade, distinguishes it from integrin or other co-receptor signaling, and identifies potential branching points or regulatory checkpoints missed by endpoint calcium or phospho-flow measurements.

ideas.title: Determine functional role of 2B4 splice variants using a channel gating analog
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ion channel biophysics, where the functional role of splice variants is determined by systematic heterologous expression and comparative functional profiling. The analogous problem—defining how splice variants alter potassium channel gating—was solved by expressing individual variants and performing detailed electrophysiological assays to isolate variant-specific biophysical properties. Adapted to the 2B4 context, the research will involve expressing each of the three differentially spliced 2B4 mRNA transcripts in a uniform NK cell model lacking endogenous 2B4. A suite of functional assays, analogous to electrophysiological readouts, will then be employed to compare variant-specific signaling kinetics (e.g., calcium flux), adhesion strength, and molecular recruitment events. This approach will define the functional 'gating' profile of each 2B4 isoform, linking specific splice structures to distinct roles in NK cell activation.

ideas.title: Dissecting the intrinsic signaling capacity of the 2B4 intracellular domain using a synthetic chimeric receptor system
ideas.description: This study aims to definitively determine the autonomous signaling capacity of the 2B4 intracellular domain (ICD) by decoupling it from its native receptor context. A chimeric receptor consisting of an extracellular inert domain fused to the full 2B4 ICD will be expressed in a uniform lymphocyte cell line. Controlled stimulation with a synthetic ligand specific to the inert domain will trigger isolated 2B4 ICD signaling, allowing for direct measurement of downstream phosphorylation events, cytotoxic granule mobilization, proliferative responses, and transcriptional changes. This approach will conclusively identify whether the 2B4 ICD possesses an intrinsic ability to deliver inhibitory or proliferative signals, or if such functions are entirely dependent on co-receptor engagement or cellular context.

ideas.title: Dual Receptor Blockade with Feedback Loop Disruption
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from neural network optimization, where simultaneous suppression of both generator output and discriminator feedback pathways in adversarial networks prevents compensation and ensures stable convergence, this idea adapts that principle to NK cell biology. The adapted research idea proposes to test if co-blockade of an NK activating receptor (e.g., NKG2D) synergizes with Ly49A expression not just additively, but through a more sophisticated mechanism: by also blocking a key downstream signaling adaptor (e.g., DAP10). This dual blockade at the receptor and intracellular node aims to disrupt potential feedback loops and compensatory signaling, thereby creating a more profound and synergistic inhibition of NK cell effector functions to enhance graft survival beyond what either intervention alone could achieve.

ideas.description: Map the full time course of NK activity in lung after influenza infection beyond 48 hours.

ideas.title: Inhibitory receptors primarily function to restrain activation
ideas.description: Explore if inhibitory receptors on T-IEL are essential for their survival or tissue residency.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2B4, a new member of the immunoglobulin gene superfamily, is expressed on murine dendritic epidermal T cells and plays a functional role in their killing of skin tumors.

ideas.title: Testing 2B4 expression stability using constitutive promoter engineering and reporter systems
ideas.description: This research adapts approaches from synthetic biology and metabolic engineering, where distinguishing constitutive from regulated expression is routinely accomplished through promoter engineering. The analogous problem involves determining whether metabolic pathway genes are constitutively expressed or subject to regulation. The solution involves creating parallel constructs: one with the native promoter driving a reporter gene, and another where the gene of interest is placed under control of a well-characterized synthetic constitutive promoter. By adapting this approach, we can engineer immune cell lines where 2B4 expression is driven either by its native promoter (fused to a fluorescent reporter) or by a synthetic constitutive promoter (driving native 2B4 protein). Systematic comparison of expression patterns during T cell/NK cell activation, cytokine stimulation, and differentiation stages will reveal whether native 2B4 regulation maintains constitutive expression or shows dynamic regulation masked by protein stability. This engineering approach provides a definitive test of expression stability beyond observational studies of endogenous protein levels.

ideas.title: Investigating the Context-Dependent Logic of 2B4 Signaling Using a Receptor Tyrosine Kinase Paradigm
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that 2B4 primarily functions in cytotoxicity activation by drawing an analogy from Receptor Tyrosine Kinase (RTK) signaling in cancer biology, where a single receptor type can produce opposing cellular outcomes based on context. The analogous solution involves mapping how ligand identity, dimerization partners, cellular localization, adaptor availability, and signal integration determine functional specificity. Adapted to immunology, the research would investigate 2B4 as a context-dependent logic gate, systematically testing how factors like SAP adaptor association, immunological synapse localization, CD48 ligand avidity, and integration with concurrent signals (e.g., from TCR or NKG2D) collectively determine whether 2B4 ligation leads to cytotoxicity, inhibition, or regulation of proliferation and differentiation in immune cells.

ideas.title: Defining functional redundancy within the SLAM family through combinatorial genetic deletion and integrated functional profiling
ideas.description: This study directly tests the hypothesis of functional redundancy among 2B4 (CD244) and related SLAM family receptors by generating primary immune cells with single, double, and higher-order genetic knockouts of family members like CD48, NTB-A, and CRACC. A multi-parameter functional screen will quantify the cumulative impact on effector functions—including target cell killing, signal transduction, and adhesion—across various activating and inhibitory contexts. This approach distinguishes true genetic redundancy, where loss of multiple genes is required to observe a phenotype, from non-overlapping or opposing functions identified in descriptive studies.

ideas.title: Employ a light-inducible degron system for spatiotemporally precise receptor depletion in NK cells
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from plant photobiology and optogenetics, where light-sensitive domains enable precise spatiotemporal control over protein function, this idea adapts that solution to NK cell receptor study. Instead of a constitutive Cre-lox knockout, the Ly49A receptor is fused to a light-inducible degron (LID) tag. This allows researchers to use targeted light exposure to trigger rapid Ly49A degradation in vivo with high spatial and temporal precision, enabling the dissection of its necessity at specific stages of the immune response (e.g., during tumor infiltration or viral infection) in a way that static knockout cannot.

ideas.title: Systematic Glycan Trimming to Map Functional Epitopes in 2B4
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology and protein engineering, where the functional role of glycans on antibodies was systematically dissected by mutating individual glycosylation sites. The analogous approach used site-directed mutagenesis to create a panel of glycan-deficient variants, followed by biophysical and functional assays to map their contributions. Adapted to 2B4 research, this involves generating a series of 2B4 mutants where each of the eight predicted N-glycosylation sites is individually removed (N-to-Q mutation), alongside wild-type and fully deglycosylated controls. These variants will be expressed in a suitable cell line (e.g., HEK293) and analyzed for protein yield, folding (via conformation-sensitive antibodies or thermal shift assays), stability (pulse-chase, surface half-life), and most critically, ligand-binding function (via flow cytometry binding assays with CD48-Fc or similar). This systematic 'glycan trimming' approach will definitively challenge the assumption that glycosylation is critical for 2B4 function by identifying if any, all, or none of the specific sites are essential for folding, stability, or ligand interaction.

ideas.title: Systematically characterize the 2B4 gene family using a comparative phylogenetic and functional approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from plant systematics to characterize the 2B4 gene family. The analogous problem—classifying and characterizing a new plant within a known family—is solved by phylogenetic placement followed by comparative trait analysis. Adapted to this context, the research will first clone all 2B4-related genes identified by Southern blot. Using conserved domain sequences, a robust phylogenetic tree will be constructed to define subfamilies and evolutionary relationships. Subsequently, a systematic comparative characterization will be undertaken for each member, including analyses of tissue-specific expression, cellular localization, receptor-ligand interactions (e.g., with CD48), and downstream signaling capabilities (e.g., phosphorylation, calcium flux). This integrated approach will elucidate how gene duplication and divergence within the 2B4 family have led to specialized immune regulatory functions.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein-Protein Interaction Mapping from GPCR Research to Define the 2B4 Signalosome
ideas.description: This research adapts the comprehensive signalosome mapping approach pioneered in GPCR pharmacology to elucidate the precise signaling pathway of the immunoreceptor 2B4. In GPCR research, solving analogous complexity required moving beyond primary partners to map the entire transient interaction network using tools like proximity labeling and quantitative proteomics. The adapted idea applies this solution by employing techniques such as BioID2 or TurboID fused to the 2B4 cytoplasmic domain in live immune cells upon activation. This will capture the full complement of interacting proteins—kinases, phosphatases, scaffolds, and modulators—that constitute the functional 2B4 signalosome. Subsequent validation via CRISPRi/a functional screens and reconstitution assays will define the core essential components versus contextual regulators, providing a complete and precise wiring diagram of 2B4 intracellular signaling.

ideas.title: Constitutive 2B4 expression enables functional resilience despite glycosylation-dependent structural plasticity
ideas.description: This fused project investigates the hypothesis that the constitutive expression of 2B4 provides a stable signaling platform, while its extensive N-glycosylation acts as a tunable structural buffer that does not compromise core function. We will determine if activation, cytokines, or differentiation alter the 2B4 glycoform profile without affecting its surface density, thereby testing if glycosylation serves as a post-translational mechanism for fine-tuning ligand interactions or signaling thresholds. This approach synergistically links the regulation of protein quantity (expression dynamics) with protein quality (glycan-dependent structure), exploring a potential immune receptor design principle of functional robustness through glycosylation-mediated structural plasticity.

ideas.title: Adapting Paleontological Phylogenetic Reconstruction to Map the Human 2B4 Homolog
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from paleontology to the problem of identifying the human homolog of the mouse 2B4 gene. In paleontology, scientists reconstruct missing features of extinct species by analyzing homologous structures in a bracket of related extant and fossil species. The adapted research idea applies this logic of phylogenetic bracketing to molecular biology. By constructing a robust phylogeny of the SLAM/CD2 receptor family across available mammalian genomes and analyzing conserved synteny and protein domains in species where the 2B4 equivalent is characterized, researchers can make strong predictions about the human gene's structure, genomic locus, and functional motifs. This predictive, comparative genomics framework, modeled on fossil reconstruction, would guide and validate the subsequent cloning and experimental verification of the human 2B4 homolog.
